CITY OF SAN MATEO
City Council Regular Meeting
September 20, 2021
7:00 PM
COUNCIL MEMBERS
Eric Rodriguez, Mayor
Rick Bonilla, Deputy Mayor
Joe Goethals
Amourence Lee
Diane Papan
AGENDA
DUE TO THE STATE OF CALIFORNIA’S DECLARATION OF EMERGENCY – THIS MEETING IS BEING HELD PURSUANT TO
AUTHORIZATION FROM GOVERNOR NEWSOM’S EXECUTIVE ORDERS –
CITY COUNCIL AND COMMISSION MEETINGS ARE NO LONGER OPEN TO IN-PERSON ATTENDANCE.
ANYONE CAN WATCH AND PROVIDE COMMENTS REMOTELY IN THE WAYS DESCRIBED AT THE END OF THE AGENDA.
To join via Zoom – click here: Monday, September 20, 2021 Zoom Meeting
To join via telephone: (408) 638-0968
Conference ID: 872 1429 8513
Passcode: 245395
CALL TO ORDER
Pledge of Allegiance
Roll Call
CONSENT CALENDAR
All matters listed under the Consent Calendar are considered by the Council to be routine and will be enacted by one motion without
discussion. If discussion is desired, that item may be removed and considered separately.
1.
City Council Meeting Minutes – Approval
Approve the minutes of the City Council Special meeting of June 14, 2021, Regular meetings of July 6, 2021 and
August 2, 2021, and the Special and Regular meetings of July 19, 2021 and August 16, 2021 and Special meeting of
August 23, 2021.
2.
Board and Commission Appointment Subcommittee and Senior Citizens Commission Appointment
Appoint Mayor Rodriguez and Council Member Papan to the Library Board Appointment Subcommittee and
appoint Jean (Liz) Foreman to the Senior Citizens Commission for a partial term ending June 10, 2023.
3.
Grand Jury Report on “Building Greater Trust between the Community and Law Enforcement via the Racial and
Identity Profiling Act” – Response Letter
Approve the letter of response to the July 27, 2021, report by the San Mateo County Civil Grand Jury pertaining to
the Racial and Identity Profiling Act (RIPA), and authorize the Mayor to sign the letter on behalf of the City
Council.
4.
Edward Byrne 2021 Justice Assistance Grant Program – Application
Adopt a Resolution to approve the submittal of an application to the United States Department of Justice, Bureau
of Justice Assistance, for the Edward Byrne Memorial Justice Assistance Grant Program for the 2021 local
solicitation in the amount of $20,515; and if awarded, appropriate funds to the Police Grants Budget Program and
authorize the Chief of Police to execute all required documents.
1 of 504

--- page 2 ---
5.
Police Department Motorcycles – Purchase
Approve a purchase contract with R.C.A. Investments, dba Long Beach BMW Motorcycles, for the purchase of
three (3) 2022 BMW 1250 RT-P police motorcycles in the amount of $109,414.02, and authorize the Public Works
Director to execute the purchase contract in substantially the form presented.
6.
Citywide Street Reconstruction Phase 2 Project – Agreement
Award an agreement to Esquivel Grading & Paving, Inc. for construction of the Citywide Street Reconstruction
Phase 2 project in the amount of $1,015,002.55; establish a contingency reserve of $102,000; and authorize the
Public Works Director to execute the agreement in substantially the form presented and issue change orders as
allowed by the contract documents and within the amount of the contingency reserve.
7.
Citywide Street Reconstruction Phase 2 Project – Amendment
Approve Amendment No. 3 to the agreement with Wilsey-Ham for engineering services during construction in the
amount not to exceed $42,000 for a new agreement total of $691,643; and authorize the Public Works Director to
execute the amendment in substantially the form presented.
8.
Citywide Street Reconstruction Phase 3 – Agreement
Approve an agreement with Wilsey Ham for design services in the amount not to exceed $403,000; establish a
contingency reserve of $41,000; and authorize the Public Works Director to execute the agreement in
substantially the form presented and issue amendments to the agreement within the amount of the contingency
reserve.
9.
2020 San Mateo Rail Corridor Transportation Management Association - Annual Report
Review and accept the 2020 San Mateo Rail Corridor Transportation Management Association Annual Report.
10.
Laurie Meadows Pedestrian and Bicycle Safety Improvement Project – Budget Increase
Increase the construction budget for the Laurie Meadows Pedestrian and Bicycle Safety Improvement Project by
$105,000 for a new budget of $465,647.75; establish a modified contingency reserve of $46,000; and authorize
the Public Works Director to issue change orders in accordance with the contract documents and within the
amount of the contingency reserve.
11.
2020-21 Consolidated Annual Performance Evaluation Report – Adoption
Adopt the Consolidated Annual Performance Evaluation Report for submission to the Department of Housing and
Urban Development.
12.
Wastewater Treatment Plant Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition System – Agreement
Adopt a Resolution to approve an alternative purchasing method and approve an agreement with E & M
Machinery and Electric dba Wonderware California in the amount of $172,901, establish a contingency for
$17,291 for a total of $190,192 for the purchase of Software Licenses and Technical Support for Wastewater
Treatment Plant Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition system, and authorize the Public Works Director to
execute the agreement in substantially the form presented and issue change orders within the contingency
reserve.
13.
Transit Center COVID-19 Rent Forbearance – Agreement
Approve an agreement with Rise Wood-Fire, LLC allowing for the payment of reduced rent during the City’s state
of emergency declaration due to the COVID-19 pandemic.
2 of 504

--- page 3 ---
14.
390 1st Avenue – Lease Amendment and Assignment
Approve an amendment to the lease between the City and Good Drink, LLC, dba Red Giant Coffee at 390 1st Ave
to assign the lease to Tong Sui, LLC, allow for a two-week rent concession, and authorize the City Manager to
execute the amendment and assignment in substantially the form presented.
15.
Borel Park Development Project – Award of Contract
Award a contract to Andreini Bros Inc for the Borel Park Development Project in the amount of $1,729,607;
establish a change order contingency in the amount of $119,414; and authorize the Director of Parks and
Recreation to execute the contract in substantially the form presented and to approve change orders within the
contingency reserve.
16.
Preschool Music Classes – Agreement
Adopt a Resolution finding that competitive bidding would be unavailing, approve an agreement with Pam Donkin
dba Peninsula Music Together to provide preschool music classes for the period of July 1, 2021 through June 30,
2022 in an amount not to exceed $125,000, and authorize the Director of Parks and Recreation to execute the
agreement in substantially the form presented.
17.
2020 General Plan Annual Progress Report Approval and Measure Y General Plan Amendments
Accept the General Plan Annual Progress Report for 2020 and direct the Community Development Director to file
the Report with the State; receive informational update on administrative amendments to the General Plan
necessary to align with a voter approved ballot initiative (Measure Y); and accept the General Plan Update Status
Report.
PUBLIC COMMENT
Members of the public wishing to comment on any item not appearing on the agenda may address the City Council at this time. State
law prevents Council from taking action on any matter not on the agenda; your comments may be referred to staff for follow up.
Public comment is limited to a total of 15 minutes; however, an opportunity for additional public comment may be provided later in
the agenda.
PUBLIC HEARING
City Council action on the following public hearing(s) represents a final decision concluding all administrative proceedings. Judicial
review may be had only if a petition is filed with the Court not later than the 90th day following the date the decision is made. Judicial
review of environmental determinations may be subject to a time period for litigation as short as 30 days following the date the
decision is made.
18.
Pedestrian Malls – Establishment
Adopt a Resolution to establish Pedestrian Malls on B Street between 1st and 2nd Avenues and on B Street
between 2nd and 3rd Avenues and introduce an Ordinance to amend the San Mateo Municipal Code to add
Chapter 11.29 (Pedestrian Malls) to establish rules and regulations governing the Pedestrian Malls.
REPORTS AND ANNOUNCEMENTS
City Manager, City Attorney and Council Members report on their various assignments and liaison roles and Council requests for
scheduling future items.
ADJOURNMENT
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
AGENDAS: Agendas and material are posted on the City’s website on the Friday preceding each Council Meeting and can be viewed on the City's
website at www.cityofsanmateo.org . Any supplemental material distributed to the Council after the posting of the agenda will be made part of the
official record.
WATCHING A MEETING ON TV: City Council meetings are broadcast live on

Comcast/channel 27, Wave/channel 26, or AT&T/channel 99.
For transmission problems during the broadcast, please call (650) 522-7099.
For all other broadcast comments, call (650) 522-7040, Monday-Friday, 8 a.m. - 5 p.m.
WATCHING A MEETING ON A COMPUTER: There are three ways to stream.
1) Public Meeting Portal www.cityofsanmateo.org/publicmeetings
2) City YouTube channel and stream it on YouTube: http://youtube.com/CityofSanMateo
3) Watch TV live stream: https://www.cityofsanmateo.org/193/Channel-San-Mateo-Live-Stream
PUBLIC COMMENTS – Can be received in the following ways (Time limits will apply) :
Prior to the meeting in writing: Send comments to: clerk@cityofsanmateo.org until 4 p.m. the day of the meeting.
During the meeting by computer: Click the link at the top of the agenda and you’ll be added to the meeting. All attendees are muted by default.
When the item of interest is open for consideration, select the “Raise Your Hand” icon and
you will be called on at the appropriate time.
During the meeting by telephone: Call (408) 638-0968 and enter the conference ID found at the top of the meeting agenda. When the item of
interest is open for consideration, select *9 to raise your hand. When called upon, press *6 to unmute, state your name and
provide your comments.
ACCESSIBILITY: In compliance with the Americans with Disabilities Act, those with disabilities requiring special accommodations to participate in
this meeting may contact the City Clerk’s Office at (650) 522-7040 or clerk@cityofsanmateo.org. Notification 48 hours prior to the meeting will
enable the City to make reasonable arrangements to ensure accessibility to this meeting.

1.
Conference with Legal Counsel – Anticipated Litigation
Significant exposure to litigation pursuant to paragraph (2) of subdivision (d) of Section 54956.9:  one potential case.
  
Govt. Code § 54956.9(d)(2): “[a] point has been reached where, in the opinion of the legislative body of the local 
agency on the advice of its legal counsel, based on existing facts and circumstances, there is a significant exposure to 
litigation against the local agency.

CITY OF SAN MATEO
Special Meeting Minutes
City Council
City Hall
330 W. 20th Avenue
San Mateo CA 94403
www.cityofsanmateo.org
 
Monday, July 19, 2021
Remote 5:30 PM
Special Meeting
 
CALL TO ORDER This meeting is being held under the provisions of State of California Governor Gavin Newsom’s 
Executive Order N-29-20; in which Brown Act regulations are relaxed to allow members to remote in due to the 
Coronavirus Covid-19 health emergency.
Roll Call
Remote:    Mayor Rodriguez, Deputy Mayor Bonilla, Council Members: Papan, Lee and Goethals 
STUDY SESSION 
1.
Diversity and Equity Taskforce – Guidance for Formation
Assistant City Manager Kathy Kleinbaum provided a presentation on the structure, composition, and objectives for 
the formation of a Diversity and Equity taskforce. Council asked questions of staff. Public Comment - there were 
no speakers.
Council provided comments noting it was a good start and understood the impacts from the districting effort as 
delaying the timeline. Discussion ensued with the conclusion that work being done on the District Elections could 
help build strategies for this taskforce. Council concurred to delay until November. 
Council Member Lee expressed interest in being a liaison for the taskforce. Council discussion ensued on the 
liaison role’s purpose is to be available if the taskforce runs into issues, rather than as an attendee of the 
committee or member of the committee. 
CLOSED SESSION 
Following the opportunity for public comment, there were no speakers, the City Council convened into Closed Session at 
6:03 p.m. to consider:
2.
Conference with Labor Negotiators (Government Code Section 54957.6)
City designated representatives: Stacey Cue, Casey Echarte, and Teresa Abrahamsohn
Employee Organization: Service Employees International Union (SEIU)
3.
Conference with Legal Counsel – Existing Litigation
(Paragraph (1) of subdivision (d) of Government Code Section 54956.9)
Name of Case: Sebastian v. City of San Mateo, San Mateo Superior Court Case No.: 19-CIV-04580
ADJOURNMENT – The meeting adjourned at 6:56 p.m.

Remote 7:00 PM
Regular Meeting
 
CALL TO ORDER - This meeting is being held under the provisions of State of California Governor Gavin Newsom’s 
Executive Order N-29-20; in which Brown Act regulations are relaxed to allow members to remote in due to the 
Coronavirus Covid-19 health emergency.
Pledge of Allegiance
Roll Call
Remote:    Mayor Rodriguez, Deputy Mayor Bonilla, Council Members: Papan, Lee, and Goethals
CEREMONIAL
1.
Park and Recreation Month
CONSENT CALENDAR
The following items, 2 through 5, and items 7 through 15 were considered to be routine by the City Council. Items 6 and 
16 were removed to be considered separately.  After the titles of the items were read by the City Clerk, the public was 
invited to comment and there were no speakers. Motion passed 5-0.
Moved:
Bonilla, Seconded: Papan
Ayes: 
Rodriguez, Bonilla, Lee, Papan and Goethals
Noes: 
None
2.
City Council Meeting Minutes – Approval
Approve the minutes of the City Council Special and Regular meeting of June 7, 2021.
3.
Ordinance Adoption – “Property Maintenance” Code Amendment 
Adopt an Ordinance to amend San Mateo Municipal Code Section 7.14 “Property Maintenance” to adopt the 2021 
International Property Maintenance Code and comply with the California Health and Safety Code. 
Enactment:  Ordinance No. 2021-19
4.
Downtown Landscape Maintenance Services – Agreement 
Approve an agreement with Brightview Landscape Services for maintenance of Downtown area planters in the 
amount of $112,840, and authorize the Director of Parks and Recreation to execute the agreement in substantially 
the form presented and to approve change orders totaling up to 10% of the amount of the agreement.
5.
Community Development Department – Classification Update
Adopt a Resolution to reallocate an Executive Assistant position to an Administrative Technician position in the 
Community Development Department. 
Enactment:  Resolution No. 71 (2021)

7.
COVID-19 Update
Receive an update on operational impacts and other City efforts related to COVID-19, and confirm rules and 
regulations issued by the City Manager acting as the Director of Emergency Services in response to declared 
emergency. 
8.
Bay Meadows Community Facilities District – Special Tax Levy Fiscal Year 2021-22
Adopt a Resolution, as amended, to authorize the levy of special taxes for fiscal year 2021-22 for the Community 
Facilities District No. 2008-1 (Bay Meadows). 
Enactment:  Resolution No. 72 (2021)
9.
Bay Meadows Block 4 – Public Service Easement Summary Vacation
Adopt a Resolution to vacate a public service easement on Bay Meadows Phase II, No.2 Final Map, Block 4, in 
substantially the form presented. 
Enactment:  Resolution No. 73 (2021)
10.
Wastewater Treatment Plant Supply of Sodium Bisulfite – Contract
Award a contract with Univar USA Inc. for the supply of sodium bisulfite in the amount of $246,923 including sales 
tax; and authorize the Public Works Director to execute the contract in substantially the form presented.
11.
Wastewater Treatment Plant Supply of Ferric Chloride – Contract
Award a contract to Thatcher Company of California for the supply of ferric chloride in the amount of $153,940, 
including sales tax; and authorize the Public Works Director to execute the contract in substantially the form 
presented.
12.
Wastewater Treatment Plant Supply of Sodium Hypochlorite – Contract
Award a contract to Olin Corporation for the supply of sodium hypochlorite in the amount of $259,755, including 
sales tax; and authorize the Public Works Director to execute the contract in substantially the form presented.
13.
Citywide Street Rehabilitation Package 3B – Agreement
Approve an agreement, as amended, with WRECO for design services in the amount not to exceed $310,000; 
establish a contingency reserve of $31,000; and authorize the Public Works Director to execute the agreement in 
substantially the form presented and approve amendments within the contingency amount. 
14.
Fiber Optic Network Facilities – Agreement 
Approve a Utility Equipment Agreement with Intermountain Infrastructure Group, LLC for the construction, 
installation, maintenance, and operation of fiber optic network facilities within the public right-of-way and 
authorize the Public Works Director to execute the agreement in substantially the form attached.

agreement and any necessary documents.

City Council Regular Meeting Minutes – 07/19/2021
Page 3
6.
Board and Commission – Appointment Subcommittee and Appointment to Community Relations Commission and 
Planning Commission
This item was removed for public comment. Lisa Diaz Nash congratulated Sheri Boles and Adam Nugent and 
thanked Ellen Mallory for her many years of service as she steps down from the Planning Commission.
Motion to appoint Mayor Rodriguez and Council Member Goethals to the Senior Citizen Commission Appointment 
Subcommittee, appoint Sheri Boles to the Community Relations Commission for a partial term ending June 10, 
2022 and Adam Nugent to the Planning Commission for a term ending June 10, 2025. Motion passed 5-0.
Moved: Bonilla, Seconded: Goethals
Ayes: 
Rodriguez, Bonilla, Lee, Papan and Goethals
Noes: 
None
The City Clerk provided a ceremonial oath of office to Planning Commissioner Adam Nugent and to Community 
Relations Commissioner Sheri Boles.
16.
Natural Gas Line Replacement and Improvements – Agreement
This item was removed for Council Member Bonilla to comment that he has concerns awarding to Express 
Plumbing over Prevailing Wage Laws and wants to award to the second lowest bidder. City Attorney Rasiah stated 
Council does have discretion to award to a lowest responsible bidder or other bidder. Council could also reject all 
bids. Council asked questions of staff. The City Manager explained the process of evaluation. Public Works Director 
Azalea Mitch explained the project and its timing and that prior incidents with Express Plumbing had been 
resolved.
Public Comment– Arturo Sainz stated these were not small issues raised regarding Express Plumbing and noted he 
is against awarding to them. David de la Torre, Labor local 261 representative, discussed the issue of not using 
apprentices as required.
Council Member Bonilla commented it is about work ethics; that there were six complaints that were filed, and 
that the next bidder in line is only $17K more.
Council Member Goethals specified that based on past precedent, we should go forward with low bidder as there 
has been no decision regarding complaints and innocent until proven guilty. Council Member Rodriguez noted the 
need to respect the process; if they happen to be found to be in violation that would be horrible, and they would 
be jeopardizing all future work with the City.  Council Member Lee said that there has been administrative code on 
the record that were not escalated to the Department of Industrial Relations; but are classified as wage theft. City 
Attorney Rasiah cited concern with rejecting a bid based on a pending case.
Motion was made and seconded. The Mayor pulled to allow for more comment and discussion. He requested 
permission for the representative from Express Plumbing to address Council. Council concurred.
Public Comment - Nick Bechwati – Express Plumbing provided testimony stating he is not going to cheat anyone, 
they have addressed every issue immediately, and noted he does comply with union wages.
Motion to Award an agreement to EPS, Inc. dba Express Plumbing for construction of the Natural Gas Line 
Replacement and Improvements Project in an amount not to exceed $250,711; establish a contingency reserve in 
an amount of $25,000; and authorize the Public Works Director to execute the agreement in substantially the form 
presented and issue change orders within the contingency amount. Motion carried 3-2.
Moved:
Goethals, Seconded: Rodriguez
Ayes: 
Rodriguez, Papan and Goethals
Noes: 
Bonilla and Lee
12 of 504

--- page 13 ---
City Council Regular Meeting Minutes – 07/19/2021
Page 4
PUBLIC COMMENT
Jimmy Apffel, commented on Bloom Energy, and asked all to join in on the Bloom Energy Stars and Stripes Community 
Run on Sunday, August 22nd to celebrate and support health care workers. 
PUBLIC HEARING 
17.
Wireless Telecommunications Facilities in the Public Right-of-Way – Amended Design Standards, Application 
Requirements and Permit Fees
Tracy Scramaglia, Senior Engineer, provided a presentation about establishing reasonable and comprehensive 
design standards and procedures for permitting of small cell in the right-of-way and the proposed schedule. 
Council had no questions and there was no public comment.
Motion to Adopt a Resolution, as amended, to approve the Design Standards and Application Requirements, 
authorize the Director of Public Works to make future revisions to the Design Standards, as necessary, to comply 
with applicable rules and laws, and amend the Wireless Communications Facilities Permit fee in the Adopted 
Comprehensive Fee Schedule for fiscal year 2021-22. Motion passed 5-0.
Moved:
Goethals, Seconded: Lee
Ayes: 
Rodriguez, Bonilla, Lee, Papan and Goethals
Noes: 
None
Enactment:  Resolution No. 75 (2021)
OLD BUSINESS
18.
Transition to Elections By-District – Updated Schedule
Prasanna Rasiah, City Attorney provided a presentation on the process and legal requirements to move to by-
district elections.  There were no questions.
Public Comment – Lisa Diaz Nash commended staff on the public outreach and recommended move to district 
elections include a rotating mayor.
Council Member Papan stated that litigation is not necessary for the city to adopt this Resolution or the schedule, 
noting the filing of any lawsuits after the action is taken is not reasonable. She commented that the city’s schedule 
provides for the most robust and informed public process; and highlighted that there moving to district elections is 
not being voted on in the electorate; and the amount of fees should not be a determinate that interferes with the 
process. The City is doing what it needs to do to complete this process.
Council Member Goethals agreed with Papan. He also asked if we could add those findings to any motion being 
made tonight, specifically not doing through litigation.
Council concurred in wanting the best path forward, honoring their fiduciary duty; noting a great amount of 
thought has gone into this plan; and that they are committed to doing it right once.
Motion to Adopt a Resolution, as amended, to approve an updated schedule to transition to district-based 
elections and authorize staff to make adjustments, as needed, to the schedule. Motion passed 5-0.
Moved:
Papan, Seconded: Goethals
Ayes: 
Rodriguez, Bonilla, Lee, Papan and Goethals
Noes: 
None
Enactment:  Resolution No. 76 (2021)
13 of 504

--- page 14 ---
City Council Regular Meeting Minutes – 07/19/2021
Page 5
19.
Downtown Surplus Properties – Selection of Development Team
Kathy Kleinbaum, Assistant City Manager, provided a presentation on the surplus properties process to date. She 
discussed the Surplus Lands Act and indicated surplus properties and their zoning. There were no questions.
Public Comment – Laurie Watanuki wonders if Option 1 is too large for this area, concerned for traffic and impact 
on the downtown. Jordan Grimes – I think the seven-story project is entirely appropriate for downtown. Adam 
Loraine – very excited to see what can come of these properties; it is important for people to be involved in the 
general plan process and continue to consider the best possible uses for development.
Council directed staff to negotiate an Exclusive Negotiating Agreement with the Prometheus/Harvest 
Properties/Alta Housing development team to explore the development of affordable housing on the City-owned 
parking lot located at East 4th Avenue and South Railroad Avenue (Talbot’s parking lot) and reject the other 
proposals submitted for East 4th Avenue and South Railroad and for the 308 East 5th Avenue (Ravioli parking lot) 
sites. Council thanked staff for methodical way in coming up with best solution and stated they are impressed and 
pleased with the recommendations and that the Downtown will thrive and be lively with more people living there.
NEW BUSINESS
20.
Report on Legislative Committee Consideration of Pending State Legislation
Drew Corbett, City Manager, provided a presentation on pending state legislation that the Legislative Committee 
reviewed. Council was asked to provide direction on whether letters of support/opposition should be submitted 
on California Senate and Assembly bills brought forward by the Legislative Committee. 
Eric Rodriguez stated that all the bills are important however to keep conversations manageable he wanted to 
limit discussions to items that most directly and immediately impact the City of San Mateo plus the ones where 
the most public comments were received; which are SB9 / SB10 / AB 1401 / AB43 / AB1238. The consensus was 
that Council would first determine which bills more than two members had a position on and then discuss further.
Council recessed from 8:51-8:58 pm.
Following indication of interest from two members, then a majority vote was required to take a position. After 
discussion the following was decided upon:
Regarding Housing and Sustainability bills: Council determined they did not need to take action on a position.
Regarding Traffic Bill AB 43 – Council decided to take action with a letter of unanimous support.
Public Comment - Lisa Diaz Nash supports all for sustainability and traffic; wants City Council to send a letter in 
opposition to SB9 and AB1401 unless amended; opposes SB10 as currently written. Jonathan New supports SB9, 
SB10 and AB1401. Seema Patel encourages support of all the housing bills. Jordan Grimes supports all of the 
housing bills.
City Manager Corbett asked and Council concurred that staff should send the letter on behalf of the Mayor for AB 
43.
REPORTS AND ANNOUNCEMENTS
City Manager, City Attorney and Council Members reported on their various assignments and liaison roles.  City Attorney 
Prasanna Rasiah announced that Council Member Goethals recused himself in closed session in the special meeting 
preceeding this meeting.
ADJOURNMENT – the meeting adjourned at 9:41 p.m.

Special Meeting
 
CALL TO ORDER This meeting is being held under the provisions of State of California Governor Gavin Newsom’s 
Executive Order N-29-20; in which Brown Act regulations are relaxed to allow members to remote in due to the 
Coronavirus Covid-19 health emergency.
Roll Call
Remote:
Mayor Rodriguez, Deputy Mayor Bonilla, Council Members: Papan and Lee
Excused:
Council Member Goethals
CLOSED SESSION 
Following the opportunity for public comment, there were no speakers, the City Council convened into Closed Session to 
consider:
1.
Conference with Legal Counsel – Existing Litigation, Workers' Compensation
(Paragraph (1) of subdivision (d) of Government Code Section 54956.9
Name of Case: Michael Quenneville
Reconvened into open session at 6:11 p.m.
STUDY SESSION 
2.
Green Fleet Vehicles – Update
Azalea Mitch, Director of Public Works, provided a presentation on green fleet vehicles choices. She outlined 
the history of the vehicle inventory to date and received input on achieving the Council priority of converting 
City fleet vehicles to a Green Fleet by the year 2030.  Staff recommends Option 2b to begin decommissioning of 
CNG plant – Fuel CNG vehicles offsite and recapture costs for investment into the transition. Council asked 
questions of staff.
Public Comment – Adam Loraine stated support for Option 2b; Carol Steinfeld inquired if the CNG may be sent 
to the natural gas grid.
Council Member Papan supports Option 2b: interested in the bridge of renewable diesel. Council Member Bonilla 
supports Option 2a: in order to transition away from CNG plant over 7 years and noted the 20 vehicles can go 
elsewhere to fuel. He also could support Option 2b. Council Member Lee supports Option 2b: citing bold goals and 
a great idea at the time but the need to change course now. Council Member Rodriguez supports Option 2b: took 
the innovative path back then but we now need to cut our loses. 
ADJOURNMENT – The meeting adjourned at 6:53 p.m.

Regulations for Outdoor Business Operations

private parking lots and enforcement of zoning regulations for outdoor business operations for commercial 
uses in private shopping centers to continue allowing schools, childcare facilities and other educational uses 
to operate outdoors during the COVID-19 public health emergency.

OLD BUSINESS
28.
General Plan Update – Overview of Draft Land Use and Circulation Alternatives
Joanna Jansen, AICP Principal PlaceWorks, Consultant, provided a presentation. Staff available to answer questions 
included Community Development Director Christina Horrisberger; Deputy Director Zach Dahl, Bethany Lopez, Sr 
Engineer; Sandy Council, Housing Program Manager; Brian Munford, Associate; and Nelson Nygaard. Ms. Jansen 
explained the General Plan process to date, the upcoming timelines, and requirements. Council asked questions of 
staff. 
Public Comment – Lisa Diaz Nash encouraged accepting the draft maps presented with no changes. Ken Abreu 
spoke to the deep housing crisis and noted the need to involve single family areas. Seema Patel stated concern 
with excluding R1 from study areas. Preston O’Connell wants all alternatives studied around downtown and 
Caltrain station. Maxine Turner stated change mixed-use to housing. Jordan Grimes commented he does not 
understand why Alternative A is being considered. Wayne Feinstein endorses the three alternatives as presented. 
Lisa Taner remarked that single family homes should not be included and RHNA numbers are double what they 
should be. Kristin suggested additional outreach and environmental concerns with this much growth. Sam G. 
stated put all three plans out there as so much time has been spent putting them together.
Council consensus was to support all the alternatives and to include Bridgepoint, Hillsdale Mall, and Caltrain 
Station areas.
Motion to Approve the three Land Use and Circulation Alternatives for the General Plan Update, as amended, and 
direct staff to proceed with a comparative evaluation of the Alternatives. Motion carried 4-0.
Moved:
Lee, Seconded: Bonilla
Ayes: 
Rodriguez, Bonilla, Lee and Papan 
Noes: 
None

Baywood High Voltage Project – Fixture Recommendation

High Voltage conversion project. He noted the improved illumination, lower energy consumption, and streamlined 
maintenance with the recommended post top style. Public Comment – there were no comments.
Council expressed consensus in using the proposed master plan and for including the maintenance of the poles as 
well. Council thanked City staff for getting feedback from the residents.

CALL TO ORDER This meeting is being held under the provisions of State of California Governor Gavin Newsom’s 
Executive Order N-29-20; in which Brown Act regulations are relaxed to allow members to remote in due to the

Roll Call
Remote:    Mayor Rodriguez, Deputy Mayor Bonilla, Council Members: Papan, Lee and Goethals 
PUBLIC HEARING
1.
Transition to District-Based Elections for Councilmembers – Public Hearing #1
City Manager Drew Corbett presented an overview on the history, upcoming public hearings and the community 
outreach meetings required to move to by-district election of council members. Council asked questions of staff.
Paul Mitchell, Redistricting Partners, consulting demographer presented the rules around districting and Council 
asked questions of the consultant. 
Public Comment:  Wayne Feinstein - concerned with moving to too many districts; he favors a rotating mayor 
among the district council members to preserve the community feel of San Mateo. George Derby “Badge” - why 
not do the charter change before the ordinance to avoid charter issues. Rich Hedges – likes at large mayor; it ties 
the City together.  Adam Loraine – commented on the need for historic redlining be a part of this process. Scott 
Rafferty, attorney for the plaintiff, expressed congratulations on the community workshop; and encouraged 
posting written comments; and stated regarding the extension, that Council could have more hearings than 
currently scheduled and have it go on for a longer period of time. He stated the need to make a commitment that 
Council is committed to doing this. Karen Herrel – This is not a huge city; stay with five councilmembers and a 
rotating mayor and then there is not problems with charter change issues. 
City Attorney Rasiah responded to questions.
Council discussed ensued on issues of districting and noted the need to be sure to get as much community 
involvement as possible. Council is still taking input on the variables and it was noted the City of San Mateo has a 
lot of community interests and support was given to do more community outreach by going to the public in lieu of 
having meetings for the public to come to the City. It was noted the second public hearing on this item is 
scheduled for Monday, September 13, 2021 at 6:00 pm.
ADJOURNMENT – The meeting adjourned at 7:30 p.m.

City Clerk's Department
MEETING DATE:
September 20, 2021 
SUBJECT:
Board and Commission Appointment Subcommittee and Senior Citizens Commission Appointment
RECOMMENDATION:
Appoint Mayor Rodriguez and Council Member Papan to the Library Board Appointment Subcommittee and appoint Jean 
(Liz) Foreman to the Senior Citizens Commission for a partial term ending June 10, 2023. 
BACKGROUND:
Per the City Council guidelines, the City Council appoints an appointment subcommittee for each board or commission 
body, comprised of the Mayor (or designee) and the Council liaison to that commission. The guidelines also allow the other 
Council member to participate as a member of the appointment subcommittee. In order to ensure that the meeting is 
appropriately noticed and agendized, this is the opportunity for Council to add their names as a participant to the 
appointment subcommittee.
The Library Board has an unscheduled vacancy due to the resignation of Lester Chen on August 12, 2021, which will take 
effect following his final board meeting on September 27, 2021. This vacancy is for a partial term through June 10, 2023.
BUDGET IMPACT:
There is no budget impact with this action.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This appointment is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is an organizational or administrative activity that will not 
result in direct or indirect physical changes in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(5).)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

None

Agenda Report
City Hall
330 W. 20th Avenue
San Mateo CA 94403
www.cityofsanmateo.org
Agenda Number:  3
Section Name: CONSENT CALENDAR
File ID: 21-5062
TO:
City Council
FROM:
Drew Corbett, City Manager
PREPARED BY:
Police Department
MEETING DATE:
September 20, 2021 
SUBJECT:
Grand Jury Report on “Building Greater Trust between the Community and Law Enforcement via the Racial and Identity 
Profiling Act” – Response Letter
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve the letter of response to the July 27, 2021, report by the San Mateo County Civil Grand Jury pertaining to the 
Racial and Identity Profiling Act (RIPA), and authorize the Mayor to sign the letter on behalf of the City Council.
BACKGROUND:
In 2015, the California legislature passed Assembly Bill 953, mandating that law enforcement agencies collect stop and 
search data on affected individuals and report that data to the California Department of Justice (DOJ). The law created a 
tiered implementation schedule, depending on the size of law enforcement agencies, and the Police Department will be 
required to start collecting Racial and Identity Profiling Act (RIPA) data beginning January 1, 2022. In advance of RIPA’s 
effective date, Police Department staff have researched, designed, and begun testing a RIPA program. 
The Police Department has assigned representatives from all divisions to help design the optimal RIPA compliant program. 
Staff have attended numerous trainings, presented by DOJ, other agencies, and private vendors offering software solutions 
to assist in the data collection and reporting components. Additionally, staff regularly participate in multiple working 
groups at county, regional, and state levels to collaborate, overcome challenges, and identify options and best practices. 
Through this process, staff have arrived at a knowledgeable perspective of RIPA, its requirements, and how to optimally 
design the department’s RIPA program. Staff have decided to leverage the RIPA program within its current Records 
Management System, which offers the most effective operational use, no budgetary impact, and accurate and reliable 
record collection and reporting to DOJ. 
The Police Department has designed a draft policy and standard operating procedure to provide clear direction and 
guidelines so staff comply with this legal mandate in a sustainable way. A beta-test group, comprised of numerous 
personnel, has tested the RIPA program for several months. This group has identified improvements and helped design 
best practices for a wider roll-out to the entire department, which is expected to occur in October, 2021. This will allow the 
entire department to gain familiarity with the RIPA program, provide feedback on additional potential improvements, and 
ensure readiness for the January, 2022, deadline. Staff have received a special commendation by DOJ for the thoroughness 
and professionalism of the department’s preparation and testing processes. 
After January 1, 2022, the Police Department expects to use RIPA data in a collaborative manner by sharing it with the 
Council, community, and others. By doing this on a quarterly basis, the Police Department anticipates identifying 
improvements in training, supervisory oversight, implicit biases, and other areas, all for the benefit of the department, City 
organization, and San Mateo community. 
27 of 504

--- page 28 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
One aspect of the RIPA program that has emerged as a potential challenge is the amount of back-end data management 
responsibilities, which are extensive. These have been absorbed by staff with already full workloads. AB 953 did not create 
a funding source for additional staff to help with increased workload, and the Police Department will have to the monitor 
on-going impact and determine if staffing recommendations are appropriate in the future. The Police Department is aware 
that potential reimbursement for staff’s preparatory efforts may be possible and are tracking this in anticipation of 
requesting reimbursement through established processes. 
On July 27, 2021, the San Mateo County Grand Jury released a report on RIPA, titled “Building Greater Trust between the 
Community and Law Enforcement via the Racial and Identity Profiling Act.” The Council has already received this report, 
which is included as an attachment to this staff report. The report summarizes the Grand Jury’s assessment of the status of 
the RIPA programs of the various law enforcement agencies in San Mateo County. The report also makes several findings 
and recommendations, and it requests a response from each jurisdiction. Police Department staff have prepared a 
proposed response letter that addresses all respective findings and recommendations and illustrates the Police 
Department’s preparation and planned RIPA program. 
BUDGET IMPACT:
There is no budgetary impact for this agenda item.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This action is not a project subject to CEQA, because it can be seen with certainty that it will not cause a physical change in 
the environment. (Public Resources Code Section 21065.)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.
ATTACHMENTS
Att 1 - Grand Jury Report 
Att 2 – Draft Letter of Response

City Council

Re: Grand Jury Report: “Building Greater Trust between the Community & Law Enforcement via the
Racial and Identity Profiling Act”

Dear Councilmembers:

The 2020-2021 Grand Jury filed a report on July 27, 2021 which contains findings and recommendations
pertaining to your agency. Your agency must submit comments, within 90 days, to the Hon. Amarra A.
Lee. Your agency’s response is due no later than October 27, 2021. Please note that the response should
indicate that it was approved by your governing body at a public meeting.

For all findings, your responding agency shall indicate one of the following:
27. The respondent agrees with the finding.

28. The respondent disagrees wholly or partially with the finding, in which case the response shall
specify the portion of the finding that is disputed and shall include an explanation of the reasons
therefore.

Additionally, as to each Grand Jury recommendation, your responding agency shall report one of the
following actions:

53. The recommendation has been implemented, with a summary regarding the implemented
action.

54, The recommendation has not yet been implemented, but will be implemented in the future,
with a time frame for implementation.

55. The recommendation requires further analysis, with an explanation and the scope and
parameters of an analysis or study, and a time frame for the matter to be prepared for
discussion by the officer or director of the agency or department being investigated or
reviewed, including the governing body of the public agency when applicable. This time
frame shall not exceed six months from the date of publication of the Grand Jury report.

56. The recommendation will not be implemented because it is not warranted or reasonable, with
an explanation therefore.

&nbsp;
29 of&nbsp;504

clarify the recommendations of the report. To reach the foreperson, please call the Grand Jury Clerk at

BUILDING GREATER TRUST BETWEEN
THE COMMUNITY & LAW ENFORCEMENT
VIA THE RACIAL AND IDENTITY PROFILING ACT

ISSUE

California enacted the Racial and Identity Profiling Act in 2015 (RIPA, Assembly Bill 953), to
highlight one of the more serious problems that can obstruct effective and fair law enforcement:
implicit bias and racial and identity profiling. By requiring “stop data,” be documented and
reported, law enforcement agencies will gain a tool to improve racial and identity awareness in
law enforcement.

Are San Mateo County Law Enforcement Agencies (LEAs) ready to collect and report the RIPA
stop data? Will the LEAs use the data to build trust within the community and improve their
departments?

SUMMARY
Professor Paul Butler of Georgetown University Law Center in an NPR interview shared:

In my class at Georgetown, I have a real-life police officer come and talk to my
students about what it's like to be a cop in D.C. And to demonstrate how much
power he has, he plays a game with the students where he invites them to come on
a ride-along, sit in the back seat of his car for a night, and the game is called “Pick
That Car.” And he tells the student, pick any car you want on the street, and I'll
stop it. He’s a good cop. He waits until he finds a legal reason. But he says that
he can follow any car for four or five minutes, and he'll find a reason. There are so
many traffic infractions that any time you drive, you commit one. And that gives
police an extraordinary amount of power, and we know that they selectively use
this power against Black and brown people.!

Peace officers” have a great deal of individual discretion on who they stop. And, as is true of all
humans, they have implicit biases. Addressing implicit bias is key to addressing racism, reducing
inequities in policing, and helping to eliminate needless or unwarranted peace officer-initiated

"NPR Interview with Paul Butler, April 16, 2021, https://www.npr.org/transcripts/987956420 (emphasis added)
" The terms peace officer and police officer are used interchangeably in this report. The RIPA Board prefers “peace
officer.”

2020-21 San Mateo County Civil Grand Jury Page |

&nbsp;
31 of&nbsp;504


--- page 32 ---
shootings.’ The connection between racism and implicit bias is well documented,’ as is the fact
that racism is present at individual and institutional levels.°

California’s 2015 Racial and Identity Profiling Act, AB 953, seeks to address potential racial and
identity profiling by peace officers.° RIPA requires law enforcement agencies (LEAs) to collect
data on every stop’ and capture the officer’s initial perception of the people stopped. This data
can help identify whether one demographic group is being stopped and searched more frequently
than others due to implicit biases. The data collection requirement began in 2018 for the State’s
largest LEAs and expanded each year to the next largest LEAs. Every LEA in the County must
collect stop data starting January 1, 2022 and submit stop data to the California Department of
Justice (CA DOJ) annually, starting April 1, 2023.

The RIPA Advisory Board (RIPA Board) publishes an annual report examining the stop data and
complaint data collected in the prior year. The report notes problems, shares successes, and
offers informed recommendations on preventing and addressing racial and identity profiling. The
report is released to the public at the end of the year or start of the next year. Thus, there is a
significant lag time between the data collection and the RIPA Board report. Accordingly, the
2021 report, issued in January of this year, covers the stop data from 2019. If an LEA submits
data identifying a potential problem but takes no action until the Board report is issued, it will be
a reaction to the data versus a proactive response by the LEA in a timelier fashion.

For this Grand Jury report, all seventeen of the County’s LEAs were surveyed and subsequently
interviewed to ascertain their progress and plans for:

e RIPA data collection and reporting;
e using insights gained from the RIPA data to improve the operation of their departments
by combating implicit bias in policing; and

The Grand Jury recommends that all LEAs, in collaboration with their governing bodies:

1. finalize, implement, and test departmental systems and processes to collect and analyze
RIPA stop data;

2. start collecting RIPA stop data as soon as possible, including earlier than the mandatory
data collection date, to gain time to test, validate, and improve processes, and begin
evaluating the collected data to identify possible signs of biased-policing;

3. plan howto analyze the RIPA stop data to improve local policing activities by “regularly
analyzing data, in consultation with [academics, police commissions, civilian review
bodies, or advisory boards], to assist in identifying practices that may have a disparate
impact on any group relative to the general population;” and

4, evaluate and consider RIPA Board recommendations and peer-LEA examples of
community engagement and transparency to build community trust and provide bias-free
policing, sought by all stakeholders. !°

GLOSSARY

Bias — prejudice in favor of or against one thing, person, or group compared with another,
usually in a way considered to be unfair.!!

Bias by proxy — “when an individual calls the police and makes false or ill-informed claims
about persons they dislike or are biased against.”!? The bias starts outside the agency.

CAD — Computer Aided Dispatch; used by public safety agencies to dispatch public safety
personnel and to respond to calls.

cities of Half Moon Bay, Millbrae, San Carlos ... the towns of Portola Valley and Woodside, as
well as for the Peninsula Corridor Joint Powers Board and the San Mateo County Transit
District.”!3 This report restricted itself to the contracting entities that are towns and cities.

a person’s understanding, actions, and decisions in an unconscious manner.”

established by the Legislature in 1959.

BACKGROUND
RIPA: The Racial and Identity Profiling Act (AB 953) & the RIPA Advisory Board

The 2015 Racial and Identity Profiling Act (AB 953) is designed to address potential racial and
identity profiling by peace officers. Key provisions of RIPA:

1. required all LEAs in the State to collect peace officer perceived demographic and
relevant data on all pedestrian and traffic stops and provide that data to the CA DOJ for
public reporting and analysis purposes;

2. created the Racial and Identity Profiling Advisory Board (RIPA Board) to oversee RIPA
implementation and guide LEAs on appropriate procedures, training and best practices;
and

3. changed existing laws on the reporting of civilian complaints (Pen. Code, § 13012) and
updated POST training guidelines. '®

This report focuses on the first two provisions. The annual data collection requirement began
with the State’s largest LEAs, which submitted their initial data, covering the last half of 2018, to
the CA DOJ in 2019. RIPA expanded each year to smaller LEAs each year. All County LEAs
are required to start collecting RIPA data on January 1, 2022 and to submit the data to CA DOJ
by April 1, 2023. The data collection focuses on implicit bias by capturing the officer’s initial
perception of the person’s race, sex, gender identity, sexual preference, age, physical or mental
handicap, and English fluency.

Unlike existing data on traffic citations, arrests, and other interactions, the focus of the RIPA
data is on the officer’s observation and perception of the stopped person’s race and identity. AB

15 Cal. Govt. Code § 12525.5(g)(2)
'6 California DOJ webinar presentation “Reporting Stop Data for the Racial & Identity Profiling Act” for the
California Police Chiefs Association. October 21, 2020.

2020-21 San Mateo County Civil Grand Jury Page 6

--- page 37 ---
953 also expanded and clarified the definition of racial and identity profiling as “the
consideration of, or reliance on, to any degree, actual or perceived race, color, ethnicity, national
origin, age, religion, gender identity or expression, sexual orientation, or mental or physical
disability in deciding which persons to subject to a stop or in deciding upon the scope or
substance of law enforcement activities following a stop, except that an officer may consider or
rely on characteristics listed in a specific suspect description.”!”

The law specifically requires that “the identification of these characteristics shall be based on the
observation and perception of the peace officer making the stop, and the information shall not be
requested from the person stopped.”!® RIPA also requires that any detention of a person by a
peace officer, or any peace officer interaction with a person in which the officer conducts a
search, including a consensual search, is recorded.!? Thus, a traffic stop that only resulted in a
warning also generates a RIPA data record, unlike previous practice where citations resulted in a
record, but warnings did not.

In California there are two main types of local law enforcement agencies: first, police
departments, which operate in cities and towns (or special districts) and are headed by a police
chief. The police chief is hired by and reports to a city or town manager, who in turn are
governed by a city or town council. The other LEA in a county is the county sheriff. A sheriff is
elected by the county residents. The Sheriff does not report to the county board of supervisors.
And a county board of supervisors is specifically barred from obstructing the “constitutionally
and statutorily designated investigative ... functions of the sheriff of the county...”2°

The 19-member RIPA Board includes a wide range of stakeholders, representing law
enforcement, academia, religious clergy, and the community.”! Annually, the RIPA Board:

e analyzes the stop data information, by LEA;
e analyzes current law enforcement training;

e works in partnership with State and local law enforcement agencies to review and
analyze racial/identity profiling policies/practices across geographic areas in California;

Implicit Bias

The RIPA Board defines implicit bias as “the attitudes or stereotypes that affect a person’s
understanding, actions, and decisions in an unconscious manner.”25 These biases could be
favorable or unfavorable assessments, and they are activated involuntarily and without an
individual’s awareness or intention. Implicit biases differ from explicit biases, which are known
to the individual and include biases that the individual may not be comfortable revealing.”©

All humans have biases. Explicit bias is easier to identify and address, if people are willing to
speak up and have a growth mindset.?” Overt racism and racist comments are examples of
explicit biases.”8

Implicit bias affects our decision making, even when we are unaware of it. Multiple academic
studies, dating back into the 1990s and repeated many times since, show that when an identical
resume is sent to a large and diverse set of evaluators for a clearly defined job, a majority of
evaluators — regardless of their own race, age, sex, etc. — offer the job to white males more often,
and at a higher salary. The only difference is the candidate names on the resume. The researchers
intentionally used names that are historically connected to a particular sex/gender, or
race/ethnicity.”

Similarly, orchestras that recognize the problem of implicit bias switched to curtained live
auditions, thus blinding the evaluator’s view of the auditioning musician’s visible race or gender.
The result: more women and people of color were hired into nationally renowned orchestras.*° A
pop-culture acknowledgement of the role of implicit bias is seen in the TV show “The Voice,”
where the coaches conduct blind auditions of contestants.*!

--- page 40 ---
Although we generally associate implicit bias in policing with racial biases, implicit bias can also
be expressed in relation to non-racial factors such as gender, age, religion, or sexual orientation.
As with all types of bias, implicit bias can distort one’s perception and subsequent treatment
either in favor of or against a given person or group. Although most police officers do not
intentionally discriminate, we as a nation have been confronted with multiple episodes of officers
relying on racial stereotypes in judging who to stop and search. Last year’s heavily reported
incidents of police violence show that implicit biases may unconsciously link African American
motorists and pedestrians with crime or with a propensity towards violence or hostility. The
result could explain a tendency for police to shoot unarmed black people at a higher rate than
white people (per capita).>”

What is Bias-Free Policing?

LEAs committed to bias-free policing provide services and enforce laws in a professional,
nondiscriminatory, fair, and equitable manner. This keeps the community and officers safe and
protected. It requires LEAs to recognize explicit and implicit biases can occur at individual and
institutional levels, and a focused commitment to examining and eradicating both. This results in
greater effectiveness of the LEA and builds mutual trust and respect with the diverse
communities the LEAs serve.*3

Why Stops Matter

A 2020 guidebook™* for LEAs and stakeholders on RIPA data collection, analysis and response,
prepared by New York University researchers, notes: “Despite the prominence of stops, there is
much we still do not know about them, including their efficacy in achieving public safety and
their impact on the public. These questions, asked by law enforcement executives and
communities alike, go largely unanswered because the data needed to answer them are
lacking.” This research indicates that vehicle stops and pedestrian stops disproportionately
burden non-white communities and the operational realities of stops—particularly vehicle
stops—pose dangers both to those stopped and to law enforcement officers.°° This research
concludes that collecting and analyzing stop data can shed light on all of these issues.37 By

The RIPA-recorded perception is intended to be the first one the officer has of the stopped
person. For example, in a traffic stop of a car with tinted windows, or at night, the officer may
not see the driver until they roll down the window. The perception is the one at that moment,
when the officer first sees the driver.*° Capturing perception allows implicit biases to be
examined. It does not matter if the actual identity information differs; what counts is how the
officer perceives the person and deals with them.

Data Integrity & Is Data Collection a Burden?

Any set of data is only as useful as the quality of its components. Incomplete and contradictory
data must be minimized to make the data useful. Recognizing the importance of RIPA data
integrity, the CADOJ produced a five-minute video on the subject in May 2019. RIPA — Data
Integrity, available on YouTube.*! A LEA that collects data without following the CA DOJ

requirements will have its data rejected, requiring reexamination of the data and resubmission.

How much time does it take to gather this valuable data? Experienced LEAs elsewhere in
California have found that data collection, on average, only took three- to -five minutes per

RIPA Board Best Practices and Learning from Peer LEAs

The RIPA Board offers “policy recommendations for eliminating racial/identity profiling” via an
annually published RIPA Board Best Practices Report. An outline of the 2020 and 2021 Best
Practices Report is available in Appendix C.

43 Grand Jury Interviews.

The RIPA Board Best Practices Report includes recommendations for policies, training, and
interactions with the community to eliminate “racial and identity profiling and improving
diversity and racial and identity sensitivity in law enforcement.’”“4

Many peer LEAs have begun implementing community advisory boards for community-based
accountability and trust building, unrelated to RIPA. These include, but are not limited to, the
University of California, Berkeley*5, Chula Vista4®, Davis4?, Walnut Creek*8, Fremont*?,
Hayward°, Salinas>!, and others. The RIPA Board notes: “For law enforcement agencies to fully
practice accountability, the community must be included in those efforts to keep individual
officers and the agency as a whole accountable. The Board will review avenues for community
involvement, including community participation in oversight, advisory, or disciplinary boards,”52

The RIPA best practices and Statewide LEA actions intended to promote transparency and trust
building that are most relevant to the scope of this report include:

e Accountability practices to improve police and community relations composed of a

systems, supervisory oversight, clear policies, and community-based accountability.>3

free policing, sought by all stakeholders*4. The 2021 RIPA Board notes: “13 of the 25

e A Stand-alone Bias-free Policing Policy which should: use clear language, including
definitions of relevant terms; express the agency’s responsibility to identify and eliminate
racial and identity profiling; include references to relevant training that agency personnel
receive on racial and identity profiling and bias; include components on encounters with
the community, data analysis, accountability, and supervisory review; be easily
accessible to both agency personnel and the public; and include cross references to other
relevant agency policies on subjects such as civilian complaints, stops, use of force,
training, and accountability.*°

e Processes to Address Bias by Proxy which occurs “when an individual calls the police
and makes false or ill-informed claims about persons they dislike or are biased against.”57
The RIPA Board recommends that all LEAs adopt a policy to prevent bias by proxy or
bias-based call by filtering out the biased information.

e Civilian Complaints practices - The RIPA Board has in- depth recommendations on
how a LEA should handle complaints from members of the public so that they are not all
dismissed as unfounded.*8

e Training on Bias - The RIPA Board also makes recommendations related to Racial and
Identity Profiling training. In California, the Commission on Peace Officers Standards
and Training (POST) creates training programs and materials for use by LEAs Statewide.
The RIPA Board specifically recommends that POST training: uses stop data findings
from RIPA reports to examine the disparities between racial and identity groups to
identify topic areas of concern for future course development; provides courses with
deeper discussions on possible officer bias that leads to a stop, how the situation evolves
during the stop, and communication skills to prevent stops from escalating; provides
guidance and discussion about the legal implications and consequences of bias; connects
recruitment academy training to field officer training and determines how implicit bias

54 Stakeholders include local governance leaders (city/town councils, Boards of Supervisors), residents, local
community leaders and organizers, to name a few.

55 RIPA Board 2021 Annual Report, p. 85

56 RIPA Board 2021 Best Practices, supra, at p. 2

57 RIPA Board 2021 Best Practices, supra, at p. 3

58 RIPA Board 2021 Best Practices, supra, at p. 5

2020-21 San Mateo County Civil Grand Jury Page 15

&nbsp;
45 of&nbsp;504


--- page 46 ---
and racial and identity profiling and cultural awareness training are being applied; and,
ensures that field training officers have up-to-date racial and identity profiling training.°

RIPA Stop Data Impact on New Legislation

RIPA data has triggered the creation of new legislation. For example, early in 2021, a California
assemblyman proposed a change in the State’s laws regarding jaywalking (AB-1238, 2021, Ting
and Friedman). The bill was prompted, in part, by RIPA data showing that African Americans
were four and one-half times more likely to be ticketed for jaywalking than whites. A ticket for
jaywalking can escalate into a violent confrontation with law enforcement.*' On June 2, 2021,
AB-1238, passed in the Assembly, it has moved to the State Senate.

DISCUSSION
Do SMC LEAs Have a Bias Problem?

It is easy to believe that biased policing is only a problem of big cities in other counties. Los
Angeles, Oakland, Sacramento, San Francisco, and San Jose have all had publicized problems
with documented episodes of their respective peace officers exhibiting biases in their interactions
with civilians.

59 RIPA Board 2021 Best Practices, supra, at pp. 4-5

2020-21 San Mateo County Civil Grand Jury Page 16

&nbsp;
46 of&nbsp;504


--- page 47 ---
When asked, SMC LEAs generally do not think they have a problem with biased policing, based
in part, on how few public bias-complaints they get. Few departments have more than a small
handful of complaints annually alleging any type of bias. All have both policies and training
designed to eliminate biased policing.

Analysis of data shows that speaking up or complaining to authorities such as the local police are
not reliable indicators of bias-free policing. For example, in Los Angeles, from 2012 to 2014,
there were 1,356 allegations of biased policing. None of the complaints were upheld.

“AB 953 expanded the type of complaints that agencies are required to report to the Department
of Justice, as well as the specific data to be reported for complaints.”®5 Complaint data for the
County’s LEAs shows that while there were a total of 43 racial or identity profiling complaints
reported during 2016 to 2019, none were sustained. The complaints were examined and resolved
by the LEAs. (See Appendix H).

Are the County’s LEAs prepared for RIPA?

As previously mentioned, the RIPA timeline for County LEAs requires official data collection to
begin on January 1, 2022, and submission to the CA DOJ by April 1, 2023. In order to comply,
each individual LEA must have processes, technology, training, and system debugging
completed before the end of 2021.

Are County LEAs prepared for RIPA data collection and submission? Do they have qualified
personnel to analyze and use the data? How will they use the data for greater local transparency,
training, and trust building? These are the questions this investigation set out to answer. The data
for all local LEAs is based on the Grand Jury’s survey® and subsequent interviews with the
leadership of all seventeen LEAs that took place in January and February 2021. The LEAs will
have moved ahead with specific RIPA-related plans since the interviews.

The survey and interview results indicate levels of preparedness across LEAs that fall along a
classic bell curve.’ Some LEAs began collecting RIPA stop data in early 2021. The majority

2015.

have begun looking at solutions to comply, and a few are lagging and relying on other LEAs to
lead the way. As of the first quarter of 2021:

+ Burlingame announced it would begin collecting RIPA data early.

+ Menlo Park indicated it purchased the necessary software.

¢ Most LEAs had either decided on their technology platform for RIPA data collection or
had narrowed their approach to two or three alternatives.

« A few of the LEAs had barely begun their RIPA preparations.

e At least one of the contracted entities did not know about RIPA nor that it will be able to
request RIPA data from the Sheriff.”

e Some LEAs were confused and believed the County Dispatch System would collect the
RIPA data. The dispatch system is part of the existing traffic citation writing procedure.
The Grand Jury did not find any technology platforms that rely on County-level dispatch
systems for RIPA data collection and question the efficacy of such a system.

« None of the LEAs had a firm plan for what personnel will be needed to ensure accurate
data collection and analysis. Some thought they might add the task to the existing
command group while others indicated a possible need for a data analyst. For the smaller
departments, sharing one data analyst may make sense.

While each LEA is responsible for its own RIPA compliance, the LEAs in SMC can consult each
other through the San Mateo Police Chiefs & Sheriff Association. The group meets monthly and
has a RIPA Subcommittee.”!

Costs

The range of technology solutions for RIPA compliance include using existing systems without
the purchase of new software or hardware, using the CA DOJ option which requires human
labor, or acquiring technology for budget and time-friendly solutions.

City and town LEAs anticipated initial direct costs to range from $0 - $30,000. Some had
existing systems which offer RIPA add-ons as part of the annual software fee, others expected to
purchase either an add-on or standalone solution. None expressed budgetary concerns.”

The County’s largest LEA, the Sheriff's Office, has designated patrol services for the
unincorporated parts of the county. The Sheriffs Office also provides contracted law
enforcement services for the cities of Half Moon Bay, Millbrae, San Carlos, for the towns of
Portola Valley and Woodside, as well as for the Peninsula Corridor Joint Powers Board and the
San Mateo County Transit District.” Its preliminary estimate of RIPA costs range from $15,000
to $250,000, depending on the technology platform.

There are also indirect costs involved in complying with RIPA. They include:

e basic startup costs of any new program: installation of software and debugging, which
may, or may not, be included in the direct costs mentioned above;

© training of officers on the use of the software, the purposes of RIPA, RIPA compliant
data collection, etc.;

© personnel costs to audit the data collection to ensure, at a minimum, accurate data
collection and reporting; and

@ resources required to regularly analyze and use the data for improving bias-free policing,

Technology cost miscalculations can occur as evidenced by the experience of larger agencies.
For example, a 2018 San Diego Police Department news report shared that the Peace Officers
Research Association of California and the California State Sheriffs’ Association had raised
concerns that the RIPA requirements, which began in July 2018 for the State’s largest agencies,
would be expensive. In February 2017, then-Police Chief Shelley Zimmerman mentioned
potential RIPA-related expenses to the city council. Later in May, council members budgeted an
additional $200,000 to cover the cost of implementation. SDPD Lieutenant Jeff Jordon said the
money was intended to pay for the development of new tools to meet the mandate, but the
agency instead received a free mobile application and program from the San Diego County
Sheriff's Department. Ultimately, the department spent $6,228, Jordon said, and the remaining

? Grand Jury interviews.
73 https://www.smesheriff.com/index.

--- page 50 ---
$193,772 went back to the city’s general fund at the end of the fiscal year in June, a mayor’s
office spokesman confirmed.”

To prevent such miscalculations and deliver on community and local-governance expectations,
SMC LEAs would benefit from early planning and consultation with peer-LEAs that are leading
in this effort.

RIPA’s Bias Free Policing Opportunity

The RIPA Board recommendations map out a path for local LEAs to deliver on the aspirations of
bias-free -policing. The 2020-Summer-of-Race-Reckoning and ongoing national coverage of the
subject”> reinforces the need for racial equity in policing everywhere, including San Mateo
County.

The Annual RIPA Report summarizes and publishes stop data findings along with
recommendations to improve bias-free-policing. Local LEAs, in collaboration with their
city/town councils and residents, can review their local RIPA data. A quarterly review, engaging
internal and external stakeholders, can identify opportunities for addressing potential biases and
course-correcting before the Annual RIPA Report is published. The appended table below
summarizes: 1) legislated requirement (bold), and 2) RIPA Board intent and recommendations
(italics).
