Agenda Report
City Hall
330 W. 20th Avenue
San Mateo CA 94403
www.cityofsanmateo.org
Agenda Number:  10
Section Name: CONSENT CALENDAR
File ID: 21-5046
TO:
City Council
FROM:
Drew Corbett, City Manager
PREPARED BY:
Public Works Department
MEETING DATE:
September 20, 2021 
SUBJECT:
Laurie Meadows Pedestrian and Bicycle Safety Improvement Project – Budget Increase
RECOMMENDATION:
Increase the construction budget for the Laurie Meadows Pedestrian and Bicycle Safety Improvement Project by $105,000 
for a new budget amount of $465,647.75; establish a modified contingency reserve of $46,000; and authorize the Public 
Works Director to issue change orders in accordance with the contract documents and within the amount of the 
contingency reserve. 
BACKGROUND:
The City was awarded a One Bay Area Grant 2 (OBAG2) grant by the Metropolitan Transportation Commission for the 
Laurie Meadows Pedestrian and Bicycle Safety Improvement Project (Project) in the amount of $987,000. The Project 
includes improving pavement conditions along Laurie Meadows Drive, upgrading existing curb ramps for Americans with 
Disabilities Act (ADA) compliance, installing new curb extensions, and adding new bicycle striping. The City awarded a 
construction agreement for the Project to McKim Corporation on May 17, 2021 in the amount of $360,647.75 and 
authorized a contingency of $36,000. The contract amount plus previously approved contingency amount ($396,647.75 
total) is well within the grant funding available ($987,000) for the Project.
Due to lower than anticipated bid prices, there was an opportunity to add reconstruction of three additional curb ramp 
replacements to the Project.  Additionally, since the start of construction, staff has determined that more extensive 
concrete and asphalt replacement is needed within the roadway and sidewalk to comply with ADA requirements. The cost 
of the additional work is estimated at $105,000. Caltrans has approved these additions to be eligible for grant 
reimbursement as they are within the limits of the project and general scope of work.  
Given the availability of additional grant funding and identified additional work, staff is requesting to increase the project 
budget of $105,000 to allow for the additional scope of work. Staff is also requesting to re-establish the contingency 
amount to $46,000, or approximately 10% of the modified construction budget. The additional budget will be allocated to 
the contract by change order based on the final quantities of actual work performed. 
BUDGET IMPACT:
Sufficient funds are available in the Citywide Street Rehab-OBAG 2 Laurie Meadows Project to fund this authorization 
request. The maximum grant amount is $987,000 on a reimbursable basis. 
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This project is categorically exempt from CEQA as an “existing facility,” because it consists of the operation, repair, 
maintenance, permitting, leasing, licensing, or minor alteration of existing public or private structures, facilities, 
mechanical equipment, or topographical features, involving negligible or no expansion of use. (CEQA Guidelines Section 
230 of 504

--- page 231 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
15301.)

CITY OF SAN MATEO
Agenda Report

330 W. 20th Avenue

Section Name: CONSENT CALENDAR

City Council
FROM:
Drew Corbett, City Manager
PREPARED BY:
Community Development Department
MEETING DATE:
September 20, 2021 
SUBJECT: 2020-21 Consolidated Annual Performance Evaluation Report – Adoption
RECOMMENDATION:
Adopt the Consolidated Annual Performance Evaluation Report for submission to the Department of Housing and Urban 
Development. 
BACKGROUND:
Each year the City is required to submit to the Federal Department of Housing & Urban Development (HUD) a report on 
the City’s performance in utilizing its Community Development Block Grant (CDBG) funds, which the City receives on an 
annual basis to address identified needs in the community.  The Consolidated Annual Performance Evaluation Report 
(CAPER) is the HUD report where the City documents its annual achievements in relation to the goals set in the Annual 
Action Plan. Through the CAPER, the City evaluates progress on its housing and community development goals as 
identified in the 2020-21 Action Plan of the 2018-22 Consolidated Plan. The Action Plan is the document where the City 
outlines where funding will be spent the following year and what goals the City would like to achieve with those activities.
2020-21 CAPER
The 2020-21 CAPER summarizes the accomplishments and progress made on the strategies outlined in the Action Plan.  
The report responds to questions asked in the template provided by HUD and consists of narratives on the progress of San 
Mateo’s community development programs, as well as the accomplishments made possible by use of CDBG funds. Several 
areas are highlighted in the CAPER:

Accomplishments and expenditures for CDBG funded activities during the program year (July 2020- June 2021)
o
Through its community funding program, the City funded the following activities:

Nine public services grants for a total of $180,000 assisted 3,354 people

Four minor home repair grants for a total of $115,000 assisted 26 households

One grant for provision of fair housing services for a total of $25,000 assisted 25 people.

One grant for provision of a microenterprise assistance program for a total of $25,000 assisted 43 
businesses, including 78 employees.

Two grants for coronavirus response rental assistance. A total of $440,000 was spent to assist 577 
people.

One grant for coronavirus microbusiness assistance. A total of $21,042.51 was spent to assist 5 
childcare providers.
o
Engineering design was completed for a project coordinated with Public Works, that includes bike lanes, 
street reconstruction and pedestrian improvements in North Central Neighborhood, for a total of 
$595,000.

Other efforts the City undertook to address affordable housing, homelessness, poverty reduction, fair housing and 
affirmative action issues.
232 of 504

--- page 233 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
o
Eight new rental units were constructed as part of the Below Market Rate Program.
o
67 new affordable family rental units were completed at Montara at Bay Meadows, 12 of which were set 
aside for formerly homeless veterans.
The CAPER in Exhibit A is a draft of the narrative portion of the report describing program activities achievements. 
Attached to the end of the narrative is a detailed financial report, as well as a detailed activity summary that is required by 
HUD.  Much of this information is generated from the HUD computer system IDIS and from internal reporting systems.  
BUDGET IMPACT:
There are no budget impacts involved in the presentation, approval, and submission of this report. 
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This administrative review of the 2020-21 CAPER is not a project subject to CEQA, because it can be seen with certainty 
that it will not cause a physical change in the environment. (Public Resources Code Section 21065.)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met. A notice was published on August 16, 2020 for a public comment period from 
August 13 to August 31, which meets the 15-day period required by the City’s Citizen Participation Plan.

CAPER
2
OMB Control No: 2506-0117 (exp. 06/30/2018)
CR-05 - Goals and Outcomes
Progress the jurisdiction has made in carrying out its strategic plan and its action plan.  91.520(a) 
This could be an overview that includes major initiatives and highlights that were proposed and executed throughout the program year.
The San Mateo community is diverse in its income demographics. The City’s economy continues to grow, reflected in part in the upswing in 
housing prices. Job growth also continues, though it is not as prosperous for low-income residents. Many residents still face housing and food 
security issues. In particular the skyrocketing rental market continues to create undue hardships on many senior and low/moderate income 
families. This was all compounded this year with the impacts of COVID-19 which resulted in loss of jobs and instabilities due to working and 
schooling at home, creating unprecedented needs in our community.

26 households received minor home repairs, home accessibility modifications, and energy efficiency upgrades, through the City’s Minor 
Home Repair Programs. Accessibility modifications are provided by the Center for the Independence of Individuals with Disabilities 
(CIID). Minor home repairs services are performed by Rebuilding The Peninsula.  Energy efficiency related repairs are provided by El 
Concilio of San Mateo County in conjunction with the Pacific Gas & Electric sponsored Energy Savings Assistance Program, as well as the 
Low-Income Home Energy Assistance (LIHEAP) program

8 new affordable rental units were completed through the Below Market Rate rental program this year.

One grant for corona virus response rental assistance for a total of $440,000 was spent. As of June 2021, the activity was able to assist 
577 people.

One grant for corona virus response microbusiness assistance directed to in-home childcare providers for a total of $300,000 was 
executed. As of June 2021, $21,042.51 was spent to assist 5 childcare providers.

One grant for provision of fair housing services for a total of $25,000 assisted 25 people.

Nine different non-profit organizations, through the City’s Community Funding Program, completed one year of service contracts. Those 
contracts served 3,354 individuals, providing access to basic need services, legal assistance for homelessness prevention, case 
management for self-sufficiency, assistance to stay in or acquire housing and prevent homelessness, assistance for victims of sexual 
assault, community engagement, conflict resolution, home sharing, and legal assistance for victims of domestic violence

One grant for provision of a microenterprise assistance program for a total of $25,000 assisted 78 people, including 43 San Mateo 
businesses

A 68-unit affordable family rental housing project called Montara in the Bay Meadows neighborhood finished construction in August of

Table 1 - Accomplishments – Program Year & Strategic Plan to Date
239 of 504

--- page 240 ---
CAPER
7
OMB Control No: 2506-0117 (exp. 06/30/2018)
Assess how the jurisdiction’s use of funds, particularly CDBG, addresses the priorities and specific objectives identified in the plan, 
giving special attention to the highest priority activities identified.
The City has significant housing, social service, and community development needs that were developed into allocation priorities through the 
community participation process.
Throughout the 2020 – 2021 program year, the City complied with shelter-in-place orders along with other municipalities in the 9-county Bay 
Area region in response to the emergence of the corona virus pandemic. As a result, many individuals and businesses in the City struggled to find 
sufficient work opportunities to pay for rent or other basic necessities. The third round of $821,000 dollars of corona virus funding awarded from 
the federal government (CDBG-CV) was programmed by the City. $521,000 was awarded to be used for rental assistance while $300,000 was 
directed for microbusiness assistance specifically to in-home childcare providers.
The City’s goals this year had a heavy focus on housing and housing-related services, indicative of the strain the high cost of both rental and for 
sale housing is having on the community. The Community Funding program was originally budgeted at the maximum allowed by CDBG program, 
but was increased as allowed by a HUD waiver to assist with responding to the pandemic.  The community needs far exceed the resources.  The 
funding priorities for the Community Funding Program included:

Basic Human Needs; such as access to food, shelter, clothing, legal services, etc.

Senior Services

Youth Services

Affordable Housing Services 
Design was completed for a new bike lane pedestrian safety project in the CDBG Priority Neighborhood, which was identified as a priority from 
community members in the Community Needs Assessment process.  Community requests to improve pedestrian and bike safety have also been 
received as part of the Neighborhood Traffic Forums coordinated by the Public Works Department. The neighborhood Traffic Action Plans 
included priorities to reduce speeding and increase safety particularly for pedestrians and bicyclists, so several related improvements were 
incorporated into the street project design. Construction will begin in Fall 2021.

Table 2 – Table of assistance to racial and ethnic populations by source of funds 
Narrative
In the City’s Disproportionate needs assessment, low-income Hispanic residents were found to have a 
greater proportion of housing cost burden than other populations. Other populations do show 
households with disproportionate needs but have significantly smaller numbers of households with 
disproportionate needs. Of the 3,697 persons served through San Mateo’s CDBG program, 
approximately 70% - 2,582 persons – were Hispanic, showing that the City’s funded programs have 
targeted those of greatest need. Not listed in the table above were 381 people who were listed as 
“Other” who identified as mixed-race, an ethnicity not listed in the table, or declined to answer.

Narrative
For most activities, the actual CDBG expenditures were very close to budget. The following were 
exceptions:
Street and Sidewalk Safety Project (CDBG)
During this fiscal year $595,000 was budgeted towards the two-year street bike lane and pedestrian 
safety project in the CDBG Priority Neighborhood that has a total budget of $1,064,000.  During this 
program year $30,896 was expended for the engineering design of the project. Construction will begin 
Fall of 2021. 
Rental Assistance Funds (CDBG-CV)
In response to the spread of the corona virus pandemic, $440,000 dollars was spent to assist 577 people 
in emergency rental assistance payments. 
Microbusiness Assistance Funds (CDBG-CV)
In response to the spread of the corona virus pandemic, $21,043 was spent in microbusiness assistance 
to assist 5 childcare providers.
Affordable Housing Funds
Although local City affordable housing funds were available, no direct expenditures occurred during the 
program year.  Program delivery of the Housing Rehabilitation Loan program was impacted due to the 
pandemic, and the new major construction project, Kiku Crossing was delayed. A Disposition, 
Development and Loan Agreement was executed for Kiku Crossing in August 2020 which committed 
$7.5 Million from various City funding sources, including the HOME program income that the City has 
collected.  Funds was are scheduled to be disbursed in the 2021 – 2022 program year.

Table 4 – Identify the geographic distribution and location of investments
Narrative
A major bike lane street reconstruction and pedestrian safety project in the CDBG Priority Neighborhood 
has begun in Program Year 2021. The scope of work is generally bounded by Peninsula Ave to the north, 
N Humboldt St to the east, E 5th Ave to the south, and N El Camino Real to the west.
The City of San Mateo identifies its CDBG Program Area by utilizing the low-income definitions provided 
by HUD. The City then further refines its CDBG area map to more accurately reflect HUD goals and the 
goals of the San Mateo community. Currently, this funding is used to provide Low Mod Area Benefit 
(LMA) through funding of the Street and Sidewalk Replacement programs. The areas included in the 
CDBG Priority Neighborhood are North Central, North Shoreview, and North Norfolk. These areas all 
demonstrate a variety of deteriorating conditions including high crime rates, high degree of dumping, 
overcrowding, and percentage of homes in disrepair. Community Development projects like the bike 
lane street reconstruction and pedestrian safety project currently in process help to alleviate some of 
these issues.

Leveraging
Explain how federal funds  leveraged additional resources (private, state and local funds), 
including a description of how matching requirements were satisfied, as well as how any 
publicly owned land or property located within the jurisdiction that were used to address the 
needs identified in the plan.
The City maintains a large balance of match funds from past projects. Still, the City remains 
committed to providing local housing funds to affordable housing projects. In August 2020, Kiku 
Crossisng, consisting of 223 affordable family rental unitswas granted planning approval from 
the Planning Comission and City Council. Throughout the 2020-2021 program year, this project 
continued to undergo additional design revisions. This project will leverage Low Income 
Housing Tax Credits, bond financing, and other anticipated sources including greater than $7.5 
million in city housing funds and greater than $5.2 million in Affordable Housing Funds from the 
County.

Table 6 – Number of Households Supported
Discuss the difference between goals and outcomes and problems encountered in meeting 
these goals.
Rehab of Existing Units
The Home Energy and Repair Program provides minor repairs and accessibility and energy efficiency 
improvements provided by three non-profit service agencies, Rebuilding Together Peninsula, Center for 
the Independence of Individuals with Disabilities, and El Concilio of San Mateo County. The original goal 
was 47 units, with an actual outcome of 26, primarily assisting very low-income households..  The actual 
245 of 504

--- page 246 ---
CAPER
13
OMB Control No: 2506-0117 (exp. 06/30/2018)
accomplishment of 55.3% of the original goal was higher than the prior year accomplishment of 
42.5%.  Staff met with each agency individually to review budget and unit goals going forward. In March 
2020, the City of San Mateo began to adhere to shelter-in-place guidelines during the emergence of the 
corona virus pandemic. This resulted in the halting of all construction activities as well as the cancelling 
of the annual National Rebuilding Day sponsored by Rebuilding Together Peninsula. These guidelines 
remained in place throughout the 2020 – 2021 program year. As a result, the Minor Home Repair 
agencies reported facing many difficulties while trying to complete projects as well as a significant drop 
in interest in the program.
The City used funds from a State CalHome program to create a homeowner rehabilitation program. The 
City entered into an agreement with Rebuilding Together Peninsula to administer the construction 
project management of that program and purchased licenses with Neighborly for administration 
software. Homeowners will be able to borrow funds at a low rate of interest for projects ranging from 
deferred maintenance to roof repair. Homeowners must be 80% LMI or below to be eligible for the 
program. To date, there have not been any loans made or rehabilitations completed.
First Time Homebuyer Program 
The program provides first time buyers the opportunity to purchase re-sale condominiums at several 
City sponsored complexes and BMR units scattered citywide. Units carry re-sale restrictions and give the 
City the first option to purchase for new eligible buyers on the waitlist. It was anticipated that six 
moderate-income households would be assisted.  The actual number of households assisted was zero. 
The level of activity in this program is determined by the number of households who decide to sell their 
assisted units to new eligible buyers.
Construction of Affordable Housing
A 68-unit affordable housing project in the Bay Meadows neighborhood, called Montara, finished 
construction in August of 2020. 67 units were affordable to very low income residents while 16 units 
were made available to individuals experiencing homelessness. Of the 16 units made for individuals 
experiencing homelessness, 12 were set aside for veterans through the Veterans Affairs Supportive 
Housing (VASH) program. 
Discuss how these outcomes will impact future annual action plans.
Future annual action plans will continue to respond to the high cost of housing in San Mateo but will 
ultimately be impacted by the amount of money available through the CDBG program and local housing 
resources.  Staff continues to work with market rate developers to provide BMR units. The current 
housing market is attractive to developers currently allowing the City to review and approve several 
projects that are in the pipeline.

served by each activity where information on income by family size is required to determine

Narrative Information
Housing prices in San Mateo continue to make it difficult for the City to provide affordable units. The 
City approved a planning application for construction of 225 affordable units in the downtown area in a 
project known as Kiku Crossing. The project went under redesigns throughout the 2020 – 2021 program 
year and construction will begin early 2022.This projectwill be partially funded by HOME funds from 
program income collected by the City from prior HOME funded loan repayments.
The City is always open to partnering with local non-profit housing developers who have high quality 
opportunities to produce or acquire and rehabilitate affordable units. Additionally, the City’s below-
market rate program continues to contribute new units as construction of new housing continues in the 
City’s Bay Meadows redevelopment and across the City.

CR-25 - Homeless and Other Special Needs 91.220(d, e); 91.320(d, e); 91.520(c)
Evaluate the jurisdiction’s progress in meeting its specific objectives for reducing and ending 
homelessness through:
Reaching out to homeless persons (especially unsheltered persons) and assessing their 
individual needs
The Housing Outreach Team (HOT) is a multi-disciplinary team, consisting of LifeMoves, County, and City 
staff, formed though the HOPE initiative that addresses chronic homelessness by outreach and 
engagement. 
Addressing the emergency shelter and transitional housing needs of homeless persons
The City does not directly manage any homeless prevention or assistance programs, it collaborates and 
financially supports a variety of programs countywide. County level ESG funds benefit San Mateo 
residents and transitional housing is provided by several non-profits. 

Safe Harbor Emergency Shelter:The City contributed non federal funds to support  Safe Harbor, 
which is the 90-bed countywide emergency shelter for adult individuals in North San Mateo 
County. Located in South San Francisco, Safe Harbor is operated by Samaritan House and offers 
a variety of basic life skills and case management services. Services accessible to clients, as 
needed, included chemical dependency treatment, mental health referral services, education 
opportunities, and basic transportation assistance. Partnerships have been established with 
other organizations to provide additional health and medical services on-site. The Safe Harbor 
shelter assisted 318 clients in Program Year 2020-2021.

LifeMoves First Step for Families: This facility located in the City of San Mateo provides 24-hour 
emergency shelter and transitional housing with comprehensive on-site support services 
including bilingual Spanish-speaking case managers. First Step serves up to 39 San Mateo County 
families on any given day. Supportive services include workshops and individual counseling to 
achieve financial self-sufficiency, measured by the goal to assist families to increase their 
household income at least 20% between program entrance and exit. The comprehensive 
Children's Program includes a Therapeutic Child Development Center for preschoolers and after 
school programs for older children, as well as customized services for each family to address any 
social/emotional issues of the children as well as the requirement that they attend school. 

Life Moves, The Vendome: The Vendome is a 16-unit SRO that was redeveloped by the City in 
2009. It has been transferred to Life Moves and now operates independent of the City. The 
Vendome provides a home for formerly homeless individuals as well as case management to 
connect individuals to social services. The Vendome encourages residents to both participate in 
regular community meetings as well take on chores to keep their home clean and tidy. 

Home and Hope: Home and Hope Interfaith Sheltering Program assists both the homeless and 
those at risk of homelessness. Through their network of over 30 churches and 1,200 volunteers 
248 of 504

--- page 249 ---
CAPER
16
OMB Control No: 2506-0117 (exp. 06/30/2018)
they provide short-term emergency shelter and meals. Home and Hope has a day center where 
participants can spend the day in a safe, warm environment, receive job counseling, and have 
access to the 'Closet' for job interview appropriate clothing. Home and Hope also aids and 
financial counseling to families that have recently experienced a loss of income and are at risk of 
losing their home. Individualized case management focuses on healthcare, financial literacy, 
education/employment, childcare, and permanent housing.  

CORA Emergency Shelter: CORA provides emergency and transitional housing for families and 
individuals escaping domestic violence. These domestic violence victims also received a breadth 
of counseling and case management assistance to help in their progress toward self-sufficiency 
and finding safe permanent housing. 
Helping low-income individuals and families avoid becoming homeless, especially extremely 
low-income individuals and families and those who are:  likely to become homeless after 
being discharged from publicly funded institutions and systems of care (such as health care 
facilities, mental health facilities, foster care and other youth facilities, and corrections 
programs and institutions);  and,  receiving assistance from public or private agencies that 
address housing, health, social services, employment, education, or youth needs

HIP Housing Self Sufficiency Program: Although the City did not provide funding to this program 
this year, staff does serve on the selections committee for entry into this program that provides 
support services and rent assistance for a one to two year term for candidates with educational 
and/or vocational training plans to find employment at a level to get off of government 
assistance payments. The program provides deep supportive services to the clients to move 
toward self-sufficiency within a two-year period. 

HIP Housing Home Share Program: The City provided $15,700 in CDBG public services funds to 
Human Investment Project (HIP) for the Home Sharing Program, as described in the Annual 
Action Plan. This provides some of the most affordable housing in the community and therefore 
allows individuals an alternative to homelessness due to rent affordability issues. 

Samaritan House: Samaritan House, as well as other local agencies, provides services for the 
extremely low-income residents that include homeless prevention through a variety of 
programs funded by others and emergency housing assistance in addition to their counseling 
and support services. This year the City provided emergency rental assistance funding for 
households impacted by COVID to prevent homelessness.  577 people were assisted as of June 
2021.
Helping homeless persons (especially chronically homeless individuals and families, families 
with children, veterans and their families, and unaccompanied youth) make the transition to 
permanent housing and independent living, including shortening the period of time that 
individuals and families experience homelessness, facilitating access for homeless individuals 
and families to affordable housing units, and preventing individuals and families who were 
recently homeless from becoming homeless again
249 of 504

--- page 250 ---
CAPER
17
OMB Control No: 2506-0117 (exp. 06/30/2018)
The Continuum of Care committee for San Mateo County implements its plan to serve homeless persons 
and families. Through this collaboration of service providers and local government agencies, efforts are 
coordinated for outreach, needs assessment, provision of services for the homeless. The consortium 
also determines the priorities and allocation of countywide Emergency Shelter Grant funds. Within the 
City of San Mateo, The Vendome (detailed above) provides services to chronically homeless individuals.

OMB Control No: 2506-0117 (exp. 06/30/2018)
CR-30 - Public Housing 91.220(h); 91.320(j)
Actions taken to address the needs of public housing
The City of San Mateo does not have a Public Housing Agency. The County of San Mateo Department of 
Housing includes the Public Housing Authority, which manages any Section 8/Housing Voucher Program 
and other public housing assistance programs countywide. Additionally, there are no public housing 
units within the City of San Mateo. Residents of San Mateo are assisted through the Section 8/Housing 
Vouchers Program at individually rented and privately-owned properties or through Project Based 
Section 8 assisted units. 
Actions taken to encourage public housing residents to become more involved in 
management and participate in homeownership

OMB Control No: 2506-0117 (exp. 06/30/2018)
CR-35 - Other Actions 91.220(j)-(k); 91.320(i)-(j)
Actions taken to remove or ameliorate the negative effects of public policies that serve as 
barriers to affordable housing such as land use controls, tax policies affecting land, zoning 
ordinances, building codes, fees and charges, growth limitations, and policies affecting the 
return on residential investment. 91.220 (j); 91.320 (i)
A commercial linkage fee has been adopted in the City of San Mateo, which provides the City with funds 
to assist in development and preservation of affordable units going forward. The fee is assessed to new 
non-residential construction such as office, hotel, medical, retail, and restaurants based on the principle 
that these new spaces will increase the number of workers, which creates more demand for housing. 
The City continues to implement several initiatives to streamline review and approval of planning and 
building permits and reduce fees.

An online submittal center to allow citizens to submit electronic plans without coming in person 
to City Hall was launched. During the height of the corona virus pandemic, the City exclusively 
relied on this portal for the submittal of planning and building documents, including those for 
essential projects such as housing.

The Online Permit Center was also utilized to issue building permits and allow for virtual building 
inspections.

The online Symbium research tool allows citizens to determine the viability of developing ADUs 
on their property.

City is conducting a comprehensive review of all development related fees to ensure they are 
appropriate.  The City Council reviewed and approved reduced flat fees for the construction of 
Accessory Dwelling units last year and approved waiver of building and planning fees for the 
affordable housing project downtown.  As part of the fee study, the City will continue to explore 
potential housing related fee adjustments/reductions for affordable housing.
Actions taken to address obstacles to meeting underserved needs.  91.220(k); 91.320(j)
The City held community needs meetings in preparation of the Annual plan that coincided with the 
spread of the pandemic. One result of these workshops was the development of the City’s Rental 
Assistance program and Childcare Microbusiness Assistance in response to the corona virus pandemic. 
From this meeting, it was determined that an additional $521,000 of CDBG-CV money would be 
programmed to rental assistance while $300,000 would be spent on microbusinesss assistance.
To address obstacles to meeting the underserved needs of the community, the City continued to 
support the regional efforts to develop more affordable housing and address the needs of the City's 
homeless or those at risk of homelessness through such organizations as San Mateo County Housing 
Endowment and Regional Trust (HEART) and HOPE San Mateo County. 
252 of 504

--- page 253 ---
CAPER
20
OMB Control No: 2506-0117 (exp. 06/30/2018)
Actions taken to reduce lead-based paint hazards. 91.220(k); 91.320(j)
The City has adopted a lead-based paint policy that is followed by all Minor Home Repair sub-recipients 
as they provide services to low-income homeowners. The City monitors each of the sub-recipients 
through quarterly reports and periodic on-site visits. 
Actions taken to reduce the number of poverty-level families. 91.220(k); 91.320(j)
The actions to work toward eliminating poverty in San Mateo include: 

Provide affordable housing: Several market rate projects citywide are in the pipeline that will 
provide BMR inclusionary units as they get constructed.  This past year, 8 new low income 
rentals were completed through the BMR program. 68 units were constructed at the Montara 
affordable housing development at Bay Meadows. The City received planning approval of the 
construction of 225 units of affordable housing on two city-owned parcels in the downtown area 
that are adjacent to a Caltrain station for a project known as Kiku Crossing. Construction is 
expected to be underway in early 2022.

Create a safe and accessible living environment: Minor Home Repair programs provide residents 
with repairs to increase energy efficiency, accessibility, and health/safety

Provide social services to help people reach self-sufficiency: Accomplishments of public services 
grant recipients serving San Mateo residents, particularly those in the CDBG Priority 
Neighborhood
Actions taken to develop institutional structure. 91.220(k); 91.320(j)
No gaps were identified internally or externally that required any specific attention to improve this 
program year. The entitlement jurisdictions within San Mateo County continue to meet and collaborate 
in a variety of ways to share innovative ideas and best practices. Most recent efforts include working 
towards formalizing our income verification requirements for sub-recipients.
Actions taken to enhance coordination between public and private housing and social service 
agencies. 91.220(k); 91.320(j)
As a leader in regional housing and other related issues, the City continues with cooperative and 
collaborative efforts with other jurisdictions in the County.  With good working relationships with San 
Mateo County Department of Housing and the Human Services Agency, the City continues to address 
related issues on a regional basis as opportunities arise.  The City also continues to work with the private 
sector through its inclusionary Below Market Rate and Commercial Linkage Fee programs to promote 
affordable housing opportunities.
Identify actions taken to overcome the effects of any impediments identified in the 
jurisdictions analysis of impediments to fair housing choice.  91.520(a)
253 of 504

--- page 254 ---
CAPER
21
OMB Control No: 2506-0117 (exp. 06/30/2018)
The City of San Mateo, along with 3 other entitlement cities, (Daly City, So. San Francisco, and Redwood 
City) and the County of San Mateo jointly contracted with Project Sentinel to provide services to address 
items identified in the Assessment of Fair Housing report, which were incorporated into those 
jurisdictions Annual Action Plans. Project Sentinel was instrumental in assisting with the community 
outreach for the new plan which has goals incorporated into the 2018 Consolidated Plan. 
Project Sentinel opened and investigated 23 cases, which met its goal of 23 persons.  It also provided 
information and referral services to 45 people; consultations to 16 people; education and outreach 
services to 26 San Mateo residents and service providers; and distributed 550 multilingual fair housing 
brochures throughout San Mateo.  
254 of 504

--- page 255 ---
CAPER
22
OMB Control No: 2506-0117 (exp. 06/30/2018)
CR-40 - Monitoring 91.220 and 91.230
Describe the standards and procedures used to monitor activities carried out in furtherance 
of the plan and used to ensure long-term compliance with requirements of the programs 
involved, including minority business outreach and the comprehensive planning 
requirements
The City’s two-year public services funding cycle is structured so that each sub-recipient receives an on-
site monitoring visit every three years, with desktop monitoring of quarterly report and reimbursement 
reviews as they are received. To ensure program compliance, NIH staff conducted desktop monitoring 
for all sub-grantee agencies and but could not conduct on-site monitoring visits for sub-grantees during 
Program Year 2020 due to social distancing guidelines during the corona virus pandemic. Six 
organizations that were recipients of CDBG funding were monitored remotely during this program year. 
Staff also implemented a risk analysis worksheet to track monitoring and to determine if sub-recipients 
need more frequent on-site reviews. 
Affordable housing monitoring includes HOME funded properties as well as BMR properties. Review is 
done annually through desktop review of required documentation and an onsite visit every two year, or 
more frequently as needed. Regular compliance and evaluation of programs is managed daily by City 
staff. Due to the pandemic, no onsite monitoring visits were conducted during the program year, but 
rents were collected and reviewed from 14 properties.
Other programs are monitored by staff daily and as needed for capital projects. This review and 
monitoring includes, but is not limited to, environmental review, contract supervision, prevailing wage 
compliance, case management, reporting, and reimbursement request review and processing.
Citizen Participation Plan 91.105(d); 91.115(d)
Describe the efforts to provide citizens with reasonable notice and an opportunity to 
comment on performance reports.
A Public Notice was published in the San Mateo Journal announcing the availability of the draft report 
for public comment on August 13, 2021.  The draft CAPER was also posted on the website. 
255 of 504

--- page 256 ---
CAPER
23
OMB Control No: 2506-0117 (exp. 06/30/2018)
CR-45 - CDBG 91.520(c)
Specify the nature of, and reasons for, any changes in the jurisdiction’s program objectives 
and indications of how the jurisdiction would change its programs as a result of its 
experiences.
Due to the ongoing corona virus pandemic, the community of San Mateo faced several new challenges 
that resulted in the City re-prioritizing its goals in CDBG funding. In order to comply with social 
distancing guidelines and shelter-in-place orders, many individuals and families in San Mateo struggled 
to find work. With a large portion of the community unable to earn income, the county and the State 
issued a moratorium on evictions to avoid widescale displacement. In the meantime, two activities: 
Rental Assistance and Microbusiness Assistance was added by the City using CDBG-CVfunds.
The shelter in place order also impacted the ability for some service providers to meet their goals. In 
these cases the City worked with the agencies to extend timelines and defer activities until safe to do so.
Does this Jurisdiction have any open Brownfields Economic Development Initiative (BEDI) 
grants?
No
[BEDI grantees]  Describe accomplishments and program outcomes during the last year.

CAPER
27
OMB Control No: 2506-0117 (exp. 06/30/2018)
Public Notice
The City of San Mateo’s
2020-21 Draft Consolidated Annual Performance & Evaluation Report 
(CAPER)
NOTICE OF PUBLIC COMMENT PERIOD & PUBLIC MEETINGS
The Consolidated Annual Performance Evaluation Report (CAPER) is an annual report that 
summarizes the City’s accomplishments of its Community Development Block Grant Program 
(CDBG) from the U. S. Department of Housing and Urban Development (HUD) that is intended 
to provide services to meet the needs of low-income residents and areas of the community.  
The public is encouraged to review the Report and provide comments from August 13 to August 
30, 2021.  Copies will be available by request at San Mateo City Hall, 330 W. 20th Ave. or online 
at https://www.cityofsanmateo.org/1091/Annual-Reports. Comments may be mailed to the 
attention of Nicholas Vu at the above address or emailed to nvu@cityofsanmateo.org. 
The Community Relations Commission will hold a public meeting on Wednesday August 18, 
2021 at 7:00 pm online via Zoom at https://www.cityofsanmateo.org/3971/Agendas-Minutes-
Public-Meeting-Portal to take public comments and review the draft report. The San Mateo City 
Council will hold a public meeting on Tuesday, September 20, 2021 at 7:00 pm online via Zoom 
at https://www.cityofsanmateo.org/3971/Agendas-Minutes-Public-Meeting-Portal, to approve 
the report.
In compliance with the Americans with Disabilities Act, those with disabilities requiring special 
accommodations to participate in this meeting may contact the City’s Housing Division at (650) 
522-7220 or housing@cityofsanmateo.org. Notification 48 hours prior to the meeting will enable 
the City to make reasonable arrangements to ensure accessibility to this meeting.

Annual Accomplishments
Years
Accomplishment Narrative
# Benefitting
2018
Rape Trauma Services (RTS) provided 78 abused children ages 1-17 with direct support. 58% of victims identified as Hispanic. RTS continues
to supports children seeking services who are victims of CSEC. In Program Year 2018, RTS provided services to 5 children who identified being
victimized by commercial sexual exploitation while residing in San Mateo. RTS provided training for community leaders and parents on culturally
sensitive support for child sexual abuse throughout the community. Training topics focused on healing from childhood trauma and the
prevention of child abuse. RTS provided advocacy during Forensic Medical Legal exams, counseling, and at risk youth services throughout the
city.

Annual Accomplishments
Years
Accomplishment Narrative
# Benefitting
2019
Through San Mateo City's CDBG grant, El Concilio of San Mateo County's Peninsula Minor Home Repair program, provided outreach to 114
families, interviewed 20 families, 6 units were enrolled/assessed and provided LEAD education and brochures, and 2 units were completed.  As
a result of COVID-19 Shelter In Place Order, 2 units could not be completed and 2 units didn't complete enrollment/assessment.  In addition, 86
families were provided energy/water conservation education and installation of retrofit measures to decrease energy/water burdens, yielding
savings for other critical needs, and provided health, comfort and safety for families. 28 families were assisted with utility/telecom bills.Lastly, El
Concilio of San Mateo County provided outreach/education at over 115 events/presentations

Annual Accomplishments
Years
Accomplishment Narrative
# Benefitting
2019
In the City of San Mateo, the HAM Program had a slow year, but finished with eight households served for a final total of about $17,000.  Due to
the Covid19 pandemic, the number of incoming calls had decreased significantly.  This year, fewer modifications per household were
completed, while more was spent per client. This is because a large number of calls came from individuals who needed a stairlift or a ramp and
these items tend to be more costly. This is likely get worse due to the increase cost of living and lifting devices as well. Some projects are going
to cost more for situational reasons and no easy access solutions. New priority for HAM for 2020 is to do more outreach in the City of San
Mateo.

Annual Accomplishments
Years
Accomplishment Narrative
# Benefitting
2019
The Safe at Home (SAH) program service area covers over 800 square miles on the San Francisco peninsula extending into the south bay. This
fiscal year SAH served 8 households. SAH projects ranged from as little as $350 to minor rehab in the $7500 range. The program emphasis is
on life safety, and a healthy and safe living environment. SAH took on the larger minor rehab programs due to existing safety issues and the
need for timely repairs.

Annual Accomplishments
Years
Accomplishment Narrative
# Benefitting
2019
Due to the shelter in place order that went into effect in the Bay Area on March 17th, we were forced to close our office and shift to a work from
home model for several months. During this time we focused on keeping our volunteers and clients updated on the situation and notified them
that we would be rescheduled National Rebuilding Day projects to September 19th. In the interim, we worked with clients to identify critical and
essential safety repairs that could not wait until September. Our staff technicians and vendors completed any critical repairs and we referred
clients to other services where appropriate while planning for a safe and successful National Rebuilding Day in September.

Annual Accomplishments
Years
Accomplishment Narrative
# Benefitting
2019
Rape Trauma Services provided 80 abused children ages 1-17 with direct support. 51% of victims identified as Hispanic. RTS continues to
supports children seeking services who are victims of CSEC. In FY 2019/2020, RTS provided services to 4 children who identified being
victimized by commercial sexual exploitation while residing in San Mateo. RTS provided training for community leaders and parentson culturally
sensitive support for child sexual abuse throughout the community. Training topics focused on healing from childhood trauma and the
prevention of child abuse. RTS provided advocacy during Forensic Medical Legal exams, counseling, and at risk youth services throughout the
city.

# Benefitting
2019
Information & Referral calls from residents to address housing and resource needs: 426Applicants interviewed/screened for home sharing and

Mateo.143 residents were in home sharing matches during the year. Average income of all San Mateo clients who found housing/housemate:
Home Providers $64,682, Home Seekers $53,828Average housing costs pre-home sharing: Home Providers 39%, Home Seekers 24%Average
housing costs after moving into home sharing: Home Provider 23%, Home Seekers 22%

Annual Accomplishments
Years
Accomplishment Narrative
# Benefitting
2019
In addition to the provision of legal information, advice, advocacy, and representation for victim/survivors of domestic violence, CORAs
accomplishments from the past year include: strengthening our response to survivors who are navigating both the civil and criminal court
systems at the same time, partnered with a local Certified Financial Planner to offer a divorce centered financial education workshop to our
clients who are considering divorce or who have recently started the divorce process, coordinated a strangulation training for law enforcement
personnel, prosecutors, and medical, mental health, and civil legal victim service provider, and quickly adapting to the challenges of increased
requests for service while sheltering in place during the pandemic.

Annual Accomplishments
Years
Accomplishment Narrative
# Benefitting
2019
In FY19-20, LASSMC hosted 103 direct services clinics for tenants located at the Superior Court in Redwood City, the Daly City Community
Center, and the Fair Oaks Community Center. In the final quarter, service delivery was via telephone, email, text messages, and Zoom
meetings, consistent with public health orders. A total of 149 City of San Mateo tenant households were assisted with responsive pleadings in
eviction lawsuits and advised regarding eviction notices, landlord harassment, rent increases, habitability problems, Section 8 voucher
termination, and security deposit recovery.

Annual Accomplishments
Years
Accomplishment Narrative
# Benefitting
2019
In spite of the disruption to programs and services, as a result of the COVID-19 pandemic, Samaritan Houses Core Services program
completed another successful year. City of San Mateo CDBG funding supported critical safety-net services to 685 San Mateo City residents,
including food, shelter, clothing and health care. For families at-risk of homelessness, case managers conduct thorough assessments of client
needs and qualifications for assistance and then provide counseling, crisis intervention, and referrals based on the individuals assessment.

Annual Accomplishments
Years
Accomplishment Narrative
# Benefitting
2019
CALLPrimose has surpassed the goals set forth for the program. Many San Mateo families who were facing food insecurity even prior to the
pandemic have now found CALLPrimrose, and are receiving the support needed to get through the immediate crisis, as well as the
repercussions that are sure to follow.  CALLPrimrose is pleased to have been able to remain open, uninterrupted and to be able to immediately
adjust operation models to meet the needs of the community while also keeping staff and volunteers safe.

Accomplishment Narrative
# Benefitting
2019
Due to the pandemic, a lot of families in San Mateo were financially affected and many were not aware of the resources available to them.
PCRC made it a goal to reach out to these residents and community members and share information with them regarding rent, food, and other
resources that could potentially be available to them. We received an overwhelming positive response from the community regarding the city
and county's resources page.

Annual Accomplishments
Years
Accomplishment Narrative
# Benefitting
2020
During the 2020-2021 reporting year CALLPrimrose was able to keep up with the dramatic increase in demand brought about as a result of the
pandemic. They were able to sustain and fine tune the new way of doing things, which has become the new business as usual. During this
period CALL Primrose served over 589 unique San Mateo households, most of whom were served multiple times each month. In total, CALL
Primrose served roughly 19,200 (duplicated) households, with 7,200 of these being City of San Mateo Households.

Annual Accomplishments
Years
Accomplishment Narrative
# Benefitting
2020
CID served eleven households in the city of San Mateo and used 80% of its allocated funding. Many requests in the City of San Mateo this
funding year reflected the need of consumers to keep their independence and the ability to stay safely in their own home. Most installations
were grab bars or modification for their home so the consumer were able to independently take care of themselves (modification like ramps and
hand held showers). With a new coordinator and slow year for outreach, word of the program were mostly spread through online and reaching
out to other organization serving the community. Calls are increasing for projects and with more of our community members becoming
vaccinatedand more safety precautions are being taken.

# Benefitting
2020
Thanks to the support of the City of San Mateos Community Development Block Grant CORA Legal was able to continue to provide legal
information, advice, advocacy, and representation to survivors of domestic violence who live in the city of San Mateo.  Of the 124 unduplicated
San Mateo residents served during the grant term, CORA provided representation to six (6) survivors in their family law case and an additional
seventeen (17) survivors received advocacy services (services include but are not limited to: legal document and court preparation,
accompaniment and legal case management).

# Benefitting
2020
El Concilio of San Mateo County's Peninsula Minor Home Repair program provided outreach to 50 families, 23 interviewed, 10 enrolled and
provided  LEAD education and brochures were provided, pending docs and 4 completed and 1 pending work. Ten families were provided
energy/water conservation education and installation of retrofit measures to decrease energy/water burdens, yielding  savings for other critical
needs; and provided health, comfort and safety for families.  24 families were assisted with utility/telecom bills (including discounted internet).  1
resident of San Mateo City was employed as Weatherization Specialist.  Lastly, El Concilio provided outreach at 72 events/presentations.

Annual Accomplishments
Years
Accomplishment Narrative
# Benefitting
2020
253 residents contacted HIP Housing for affordable housing, Home sharing, and community resources to develop a plan to meet their housing
needs.91 residents and 24 persons who work in San Mateo applied to the Home Sharing program and were provided affordable housing
resources, home sharing referrals, and connection to community resources.4 Income eligible Seekers were matched in a Home Sharing
arrangement as well as 2 Home Providers. The home seekers worked at Trader Joes as a clerk, Uber/Lyft driver, and as a warehouse manager.
Their average income was $32,595. On average, they were able to keep their housing costs at 34% of their income by Home Sharing.95 San
Mateo residents remain in their Home Sharing match made in a previous fiscal year.

# Benefitting
2020

organizations like Edgewood and the First Five, Help me Grow Systems Change Group. These collaborations keeps IEP in touch with education
issues facing families in San Mateo, and has yielded presentations and future workshop opportunities.

Annual Accomplishments
Years
Accomplishment Narrative
# Benefitting
2020
Rape Trauma Services provided 82 abused children ages 1-17 with direct support. 50% of victims identified as Hispanic. RTS continues to
supports children seeking services who are victims of CSEC. In FY 2020/21, RTS provided services to 6 children who identified being victimized
by commercial sexualexploitation while residing in San Mateo. RTS provided training for community leaders and parents on culturally sensitive
support for child sexual abuse throughout the community. Training topics focused on healing from childhood trauma and the prevention of child
abuse. RTS provided advocacy duringForensic Medical Legal exams, counseling, and at risk youth services throughout the city.

Annual Accomplishments
Years
Accomplishment Narrative
# Benefitting
2020
The Safe at Home (SAH) program service area covers over 800 square miles on the San Francisco peninsula extending into the south bay. This
fiscal year SAH served 197 people in 103 homes from Daly City to Sunnyvale.SAH projects ranged from as little as 750$ to minor rehab in the
8000$ range. The program emphasis is on life safety, and a healthy and safe living environment. SAH took on the larger minor rehab programs
due to existing safety issues and the need for timely repairs.

CITY OF SAN MATEO
CITY OF SAN MATEO
Agenda Report
City Hall
330 W. 20th Avenue
San Mateo CA 94403
www.cityofsanmateo.org
Agenda Number:  12
Section Name: CONSENT CALENDAR
File ID: 21-5092
TO:
City Council
FROM:
Drew Corbett, City Manager
PREPARED BY:
Public Works Department
MEETING DATE:
September 20, 2021 
SUBJECT:
Wastewater Treatment Plant Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition System – Agreement
RECOMMENDATION:
Adopt a Resolution to approve an alternative purchasing method and approve an agreement with E & M Machinery and 
Electric dba Wonderware California in the amount of $172,901, establish a contingency for $17,291 for a total of $190,192 
for the purchase of Software Licenses and Technical Support for Wastewater Treatment Plant Supervisory Control and 
Data Acquisition system, and authorize the Public Works Director to execute the agreement in substantially the form 
presented and issue change orders within the contingency reserve. 
BACKGROUND:
The wastewater treatment plant (WWTP) uses a Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA) system to monitor and 
control equipment and processes at both the WWTP and Dale Avenue Pump Station. As part of the Clean Water Program’s 
SCADA Merger Project (Project), the control systems for the WWTP, sewer collection system, and the Underground Flow 
Equalization Flow System will be integrated and operate as one. E&M Machinery and Electric dba Wonderware California 
is currently working with the Clean Water Program to integrate and expand the SCADA system.
The SCADA system is critical to maintain the operation of these facilities and must be monitored and updated as necessary.
The upgrade, integration, and expansion of the SCADA system will allow for greater security and redundancy within the 
system. Cybersecurity represents a serious threat, and one of the recommended best practices is to keep software 
upgraded to latest versions and have all current patches. 
San Mateo Municipal Code section 3.60.050(b) authorizes procurement without competitive bidding procedures when 
calling for bids is impracticable, unavailing, or impossible. An agreement with E&M Machinery and Electric dba 
Wonderware California meets these criteria. In addition, qualifying another vendor that satisfactorily meets the 
requirements would entail changing the entire operating system at increased time and cost. Selecting a different vendor 
would incur a redesign of the entire SCADA system and updating SCADA terminals, which would take a considerable 
amount of time to complete. Therefore, staff recommends approving the contract with E&M Machinery and Electric dba 
Wonderware California. 
BUDGET IMPACT:
Sufficient funds are available for the cost of the software license and support within the following budgets: WWTP 
Maintenance (20%), Sewer Maintenance (3%), and Clean Water Program Collection System SCADA project (77%) budget. 
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This agreement is exempt from CEQA in that it can be seen with certainty that there is no possibility this activity may have 
a significant effect on the environment, because this activity relates to software technology license and support without 
340 of 504

--- page 341 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
any necessary physical changes that may impact the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15061(b)(3).)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.
ATTACHMENTS
Att 1 – Proposed Resolution
Att 2 – Agreement
STAFF CONTACT  
Rob Learmonth, Maintenance Superintendent
rlearmonth@cityofsanmateo.org
(650) 522-7312
Michael Sutter, Interim Wastewater Treatment Plant Manager
msutter@cityofsanmateo.org
(650) 522-7376

--- page 342 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
RESOLUTION NO. 93 (2021)
APPROVING ALTERNATIVE PURCHASING PROCEDURE FOR AN AGREEMENT WITH E&M MACHINERY AND 
ELECTRIC DBA WONDERWARE CALIFORNIA FOR SOFTWARE LICENSES, MAINTENANCE AND SUPPORT
WHEREAS, the Wastewater Treatment Plant uses a Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA) 
system to monitor and control equipment and processes at both the Wastewater Treatment Plant and Dale 
Avenue pump station; and 
WHEREAS, the Clean Water Program’s SCADA Merger Project, the SCADA systems for the Wastewater 
Treatment Plant, sewer collection system, and the underground flow equalization flow system will be integrated 
to operate as one system; and
WHEREAS, the upgrade, integration and expansion of the SCADA system will allow for greater security 
and redundancy within the system; and
WHEREAS, the system needs to be maintained and updated; and
WHEREAS, E&M Machinery and Electric dba Wonderware California is integrated in the Wastewater 
Treatment Plant and Dale Ave pump station; and
WHEREAS, qualifying another vendor that satisfactorily meets the requirements would entail changing 
the entire operating system at increases time and cost; and 
WHEREAS, in accordance with Municipal Code section 3.60.050 (b) agreements may be awarded without 
competitive bids if calling for bids would be impracticable, unavailing or impossible and the City Council sets 
forth findings designating the reasons; and
WHEREAS, City staff recommends approval of the agreement with E&M Machinery and Electric dba 
Wonderware California for SCADA software licenses, maintenance, and support.
NOW, THEREFORE, THE CITY COUNCIL OF THE CITY OF SAN MATEO, CALIFORNIA, HEREBY FINDS and 
RESOLVES that:
1.
This Council action is exempt from CEQA, because it can be seen with certainty that there is no 
possibility this activity may have a significant effect on the environment, because this activity relates 
to software technology license and support without any necessary physical changes that may impact 
the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15061(b)(3).)
2.
In accordance with Municipal Code section 3.60.050 (b), calling for bids would be impracticable 
because:  
a)
E&M Machinery and Electric dba Wonderware California provides services necessary to 
expand to other City systems that coordinate with the Wastewater Treatment Plant; and 
342 of 504

--- page 343 ---
Resolution No. 93 (2021)
Page 2 of 2
WWTP SCADA Agreement
b)
Approval of another vendor could delay the project and cause interruptions of the processes 
of the Wastewater Treatment Plant.
3.
The agreement with E&M Machinery and Electric dba Wonderware California, for SCADA software 
licenses, maintenance, and support, in the amount not to exceed of $190,192, is approved.
4.
The Public Works Director is authorized to execute the agreement in substantially the form 
submitted, on behalf of the City.

CITY OF SAN MATEO 
AGREEMENT WITH E&M MACHINERY AND
ELECTRIC DBA WONDERWARE CALIFORNIA FOR 
MASTER MAINTENANCE AND SUPPORT AGREEMENT
This Software Maintenance and Support Services Agreement (hereinafter “Agreement”) made as of 
this day of
, is entered into by and between E&M Machinery and Electric 
dba Wonderware California (“Vendor”), a Corporation, and the City of San Mateo and its Affiliates 
(“Customer”), a municipal corporation in the state of California.
RECITALS:
A. Vendor is making available Software Maintenance and Support services for certain of Customer's 
software; and
B.
Customer has a license to use said software (“Software”) on a perpetual basis; and
C. Customer has selected Vendor to provide such Software Maintenance and Support Services;
NOW, THEREFORE, the parties agree to the following terms and conditions:
SECTION 1 ‐ CONSTRUCTION AND DEFINITIONS
1.1
Construction.
1.1.1
As used in this Agreement, unless specifically stated otherwise references to: (a) “including” 
shall mean “including without limitation” and (b) “or” shall mean “either or both.” Unless specifically 
stated otherwise, references to “Sections” or “Exhibits” shall be deemed to be references to the 
corresponding sections or exhibits in this Agreement.
1.1.2
Title and paragraph headings contained in the Agreement are for convenient reference and do 
not constitute part of the Agreement and shall not affect the interpretation of the Agreement.
1.1.3
This Agreement shall be binding upon and inure to the benefit of the parties to the Agreement 
and their respective successors and assigns.
1.2
Definitions. Capitalized terms in this Agreement, not otherwise defined, shall have the 
following meaning:
344 of 504

--- page 345 ---
1.2.1
“Acceptance” means that the Customer has accepted in writing that the Software is performing 
in accordance with its Documentation (as defined below). Within sixty (60) days of the Go Live Date (as 
defined below) Customer will create a list of Defects (as defined below) which must be corrected  
before Acceptance, except for minor or inconsequential errors, and such Acceptance shall not be 
unreasonably withheld.
1.2.2
“Affiliate” means any person or entity that is controlled by, controls or is under common 
control with Customer or in which Customer has the authority to manage the operations, policy, 
affairs, reporting, information processing or accounting responsibilities of such entity.
1.2.3
“Applicable Specifications” means the functional and operational characteristics of the Product 
as described in the Vendor’s current published documentation, specifications, literature, the written 
proposals, if any, made by Vendor to Customer, and any additional specifications set forth in the 
Product Schedule.
1.2.4
“Change Order” is a mutual agreement in writing by both Parties changing the scope of the 
project. The Change Order will include information such as but not limited to the date executed, due 
date, scope of work, assumptions, agreed upon cost, hours and rates, job responsibilities, deliverables, 
verification and acceptance testing, payment terms, requestor, and approving parties.
1.2.5
“Commentary” means any communications clarifying system functionality such as but not 
limited to presentations, whitepapers, and clarification emails and can be in various formats such as 
verbal communications, CD, DVD, paper copy, electronic file and PDF file.
1.2.6
“Confidential Information” or “Proprietary Information” means all information and materials 
furnished by either party in connection with the Products to be provided under this Agreement, 
including, without limitation, each Product, all information transmitted in writing, orally, visually, or on 
magnetic or other media, and including, database data, organizational information, system 
architecture, software, graphics, programs, designs, ideas, concepts, flow charts, diagrams, computer 
programs, methods, research, and any other personal or intellectual property relating to either party, 
its respective parent, subsidiaries, or affiliates. The following shall not be considered Confidential or 
Proprietary Information: (i) any information in the public domain at the time of its communication 
thereof to either party by the other party; (ii) any information which enters the public domain, through 
no fault of the receiving party subsequent to the time of its communication to the receiving party; (iii) 
any information which is obtained in good faith by either party from a third party, provided such third 
party is not bound by a confidentiality agreement with Vendor or Customer, as applicable; (iv) 
information that was already known to that party prior to the date of disclosure to that party; (v) 
information which the receiving party establishes was developed independently of confidential or 
proprietary information furnished to it; and (vi) information that is required to be disclosed to any 
court, government authority or regulatory authority or pursuant to requirement of any state or federal 
law.
1.2.7
“Customization” means but is not limited to all code, software modifications or computer 
programming to the licensed Software developed or modified under the terms of this Agreement or 
related Change Order or Statement of Work.
345 of 504

--- page 346 ---
1.2.8
“Defect” means (i) an omission in software code causing the software to operate such that it is 
not in compliance with the Applicable Specifications or (ii) the media on which the Product is delivered 
is damaged or corrupted such that the software does not load properly, or (iii) the Documentation 
contains gaps and/or erroneous information with regard to the Product.
1.2.9
“Documentation” means the Vendor’s written proposal to the Customer, user manuals, 
collateral material (such as brochures), systems guides, technical guides, training documents, Vendor’s 
Web site documents and information, mutually agreed upon Statement of Work, Commentary, and 
Supporting Documentation (see definition below) related to the Covered Software (as defined below).
1.2.10 “Effective Date” means the date that this Agreement takes effect, which unless otherwise 
stated, will be the date set forth on Page One of this Agreement upon being signed by duly authorized 
representatives of Vendor and Customer.
1.2.11 “Enhancement” means any new Software program, or part thereof, or modification to an 
existing program which may be provided by Vendor to provide new functionality, correct known 
problems or Defects, or improve the Software and which are generally made available to Vendor 
Customers.
1.2.12 “Functional Specification” means the specific business functions that the Covered Software (as 
defined below) shall provide as described in the Documentation.
1.2.13 “Go Live Date” is the date of the first production use of the licensed Software by Customer 
where the Customer is using the Software to manage its normal business operations. Prototype 
development and testing, data conversion and testing, initial system training, and conference room 
pilot operations and testing take place before the Go Live Date.
1.2.14 “Maintenance and Support” means the Professional Services, Enhancements, and Updates 
provided by Vendor to City pursuant to this Agreement and the included Statement of Work.
1.2.15
“Maintenance and Support Fee” means the fees which shall be assessed to Customer by 
Vendor for Maintenance and Support as defined herein.
1.2.16
“Module” means a subset of the Software that relates to a specific set of related business 
functions.
1.2.17 “Product” means the Vendor’s computer software programs (“Software”) identified in the 
Product Schedule, and related Documentation, manuals, utilities and other related items and 
information. Product shall also include each and every Release (as defined below), patch, upgrade, 
version or Product correction available from Vendor during the term of each Maintenance and Support 
Period.
346 of 504

--- page 347 ---
1.2.18
“Professional Services” means all services provided by Vendor which are primarily intellectual 
and mental, and performed by qualified personnel. Such services may include without limitation 
project planning, consulting, project management, data conversion, training, programming, and 
development of Customizations.
1.2.19 “Program Error” means there is incongruence between the behavior of the Software and the 
documented functionality and operation of the Software or any other problem or irregularity which 
affects the material functionality of the Software.
1.2.20 “Software” means the Vendor’s computer software program(s) licensed by Customer and 
identified in the Product Schedule.
1.2.21 “Statement of Work” means a document which describes, in detail, the services to be provided 
by Vendor and its Subcontractors (as defined below), implementation schedule, responsibilities of 
Customer and Vendor, description and schedule of training, description and schedule for development 
of Project Systems, Acceptance plans and protocols, and contingencies.
1.2.22 “Subcontractor” means business entity with which City or Vendor may contract with to perform 
certain functions identified in the Statement of Work.
1.2.23 “Supporting Documentation” means all materials, in print or electronic form, related to the 
Software and Customizations which includes: technical specifications, technical manuals, user manuals, 
user guides, abbreviated user guides, and all other training, reference, or support materials which are 
provided under the terms of this Agreement.
1.2.24
"System Software" means software, which may include Third Party Software and related 
documentation, that control hardware functions, provide fundamental relational database 
management and standard query language capability, provide an application development language 
and environment, and enable web and internet access to the Applications Software, Customizations, 
and Third Party Software, which are commonly referred to as operating systems and database 
management systems, database engines, compilers, programming languages, debugging utilities, 
backup and restoration utilities, and web management utilities.
1.2.25
“Technical Requirements” means the detailed description of the hardware, networking, and 
System Software environment required to allow Customer full and productive use of Product and 
System, pursuant to the Functional Specifications and Documentation.
1.2.26 “Third Party Software” means all software required by, or related to, the Covered Software and 
Customizations that are necessary and required to allow the such Software and Customizations to 
provide the Functional Requirements and which is provided by a third party directly to Customer.
